Analysing the loss-alignment relationship on Qwen3-8B, we find that final log training loss is a strong predictor of alignment, and that stratifying by optimiser captures nearly all the residual variance. Training dynamics reveal that each optimiser follows a different trajectory through loss-alignment space, and that after significant training, the optimiser becomes more important than training loss as a predictor of alignment. Muon, the adaptive optimiser that preserves alignment the best, implicitly regularises for a more uniform distribution of singular values of the LoRA adapter. We evaluate this insight by training with an additional loss term that incentivises a flatter singular value spectrum, and find that this substantially recovers alignment for the more EM-prone adaptive optimisers (Adam and Lion), with negligible cost to training loss. These results identify optimiser choice as a key factor in EM severity, but show that spectral regularisation can substantially mitigate the effects of EM-prone optimisers.

</details>


### [215] [Calibration, Not Compilation: Detecting and Repairing Misspecified Probabilistic Programs Written by Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31630)
*Jian Xu,Delu Zeng,John Paisley,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that for probabilistic programs written by LLMs, statistical correctness (calibration) matters far more than mere compilation or unit tests. The authors show that a calibration oracle detects misspecifications with 0.97 AUC, that calibration-guided repair outperforms unit-test feedback (which can actually worsen outcomes), and that 15-47% of runnable LLM-generated programs are statistically wrong despite passing all tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models can write probabilistic programs that compile and pass unit tests but are statistically invalid (e.g., wrong likelihood, invalid priors, pathological parameterization). Traditional software testing is insufficient; the right verifier should be the Bayesian workflow (posterior predictive checks, simulation-based calibration, sampler diagnostics).

Method: The paper studies a calibration oracle along three axes: (1) Detection — benchmarking 14 misspecification types across 10 model families (200 instances); (2) Repair — using calibration feedback in an LLM repair loop across 15 models; (3) Reality — testing on real LLM-written programs from neutral briefs.

Result: Detection: AUC 0.97 (88% at 2% FPR with reference program; 62-78% reference-free). Repair: Calibration significantly outperforms unit-test feedback, which is significantly worse than no feedback. Reality: 15-47% of runnable LLM-generated programs are statistically misspecified; calibration-guided repair beats LLM-as-judge and other methods.

Conclusion: For probabilistic programs, correctness is calibration, not compilation. Unit tests can induce false confidence that suppresses repair, while calibration provides a principled statistical verifier.

Abstract: Language models increasingly write probabilistic programs (in NumPyro, Stan, or Pyro), but a program that compiles, runs, and passes every unit test can still be \emph{statistically} wrong -- a Gaussian likelihood for heavy-tailed data, a Poisson for over-dispersed counts, an invalid prior support, or a pathological parameterization. The right verifier is therefore not a test suite but the Bayesian workflow itself: posterior predictive checks, simulation-based calibration, sampler diagnostics ($\hat R$, divergences, ESS), and held-out predictive density. We study this calibration oracle along three axes. \textbf{Detection:} on a benchmark of $14$ misspecification types across $10$ model families ($200$ instances), it flags the bug with AUC $0.97$ ($88\%$ at $2\%$ FPR \emph{when handed the correct reference program, an upper bound}) -- and a fully \emph{reference-free} version that uses no correct program reaches $62$--$78\%$ (the upper figure from a small automated model search), versus $0\%$ for a unit-test oracle. \textbf{Repair:} used as feedback in an LLM repair loop across fifteen models, calibration significantly outperforms unit-test feedback -- which is itself \emph{significantly worse than no feedback at all}, a passing test inducing false confidence that suppresses repair -- and improves over no feedback on strong-but-unsaturated models (GPT-5.1 $33{\to}92\%$, Claude $75{\to}100\%$; paired McNemar, $n{=}228$). \textbf{Reality:} on programs LLMs write from scratch for neutral briefs, $15$--$47\%$ of runnable ones are statistically misspecified (unit tests catch none), and calibration-guided repair significantly beats LLM-as-judge review, a Bayesian-workflow checklist, and data-summary self-debug. Across all three, the lesson is the same: for probabilistic programs, correctness is calibration, not compilation.

</details>


### [216] [ECHO: Prune to act, trace to learn with selective turn memory in agentic RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31650)
*Zijun Xie,Binbin Zheng,Enlei Gong,Jihua Liu,Yuyang You,Lingfeng Liu,Jiayao Tang,Guanqun Zhao,Aoqi Hu,Zeyu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ECHO proposes a selective turn-memory framework that addresses history collapse and enables traceable RL by compressing turns into compact records, reconstructing bounded contexts via selection, and routing positive credit to supporting evidence through source indices. Outperforms GRPO and SUPO on BrowseComp-Plus (43.4% vs 28.9%/36.1%) with fewer turns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing context-management methods for long-horizon language agents (truncation, summarization, memory selection) cause two coupled issues: (1) history collapse—fine-grained evidence is progressively lost as turns grow; (2) no traceable learning—once original turns are not source-addressable, outcome-based RL cannot explicitly align policy updates with the evidence that supported successful answers.

Method: ECHO (selective turn-memory framework): (1) Compresses each completed environment turn into a compact memory record; (2) Reconstructs bounded policy contexts by selectively retrieving from these records; (3) Reuses selected source indices to route positive outcome credit back to the evidence and selection actions that supported successful answers.

Result: On BrowseComp-Plus, ECHO achieves 43.4% held-out accuracy, surpassing GRPO (28.9%) and SUPO (36.1%), while using fewer turns and lower trajectory volume. The trained policy also improves zero-shot generalization across multi-objective QA, code generation, and deep information-seeking benchmarks on both dense and MoE backbones.

Conclusion: ECHO effectively addresses the dual challenges of history collapse and untraceable RL for long-horizon language agents, achieving superior accuracy with greater efficiency, and demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Long-horizon language agents must repeatedly interact with tools, accumulate evidence, and make decisions under bounded context windows. Existing context-management methods make such rollouts feasible by truncating distant history, folding past turns into summaries, or selecting compact memory states. However, these breakthroughs introduce two coupled limitations. First, as the number of turns grows, historical observations are progressively removed or collapsed into compressed states, making it harder for the policy to reuse fine-grained evidence. Second, once the original turns are no longer source-addressable, outcome-based RL loses an explicit path for aligning policy updates with the evidence that supported a successful final answer. To this end, we propose ECHO, a selective turn-memory framework that jointly addresses history collapse and traceable learning through source-indexed reconstruction. Specifically, ECHO compresses each completed environment turn into a compact memory record, reconstructs bounded policy contexts by selecting from these records, and reuses the selected source indices to route positive outcome credit to the evidence and selection actions that support successful answers. On BrowseComp-Plus, ECHO reaches 43.4% held-out accuracy, outperforming GRPO (28.9%) and the rolling-summary baseline SUPO (36.1%), while using fewer turns and lower trajectory volume than SUPO (Figure 1). Additionally, the trained policy improves zero-shot generalization across multi-objective QA, code generation, and deep information-seeking benchmarks on both dense and MoE backbones.

</details>


### [217] [Improving Certified Robustness via Adversarial Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31653)
*Matteo Melis,Jesus Martinez Del Rincon,Vishal Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AD-CERT combines adversarial distillation with Interval Bound Propagation to achieve state-of-the-art certified robustness, improving certified accuracy by distilling logit-level adversarial information from an empirically robust teacher.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing certified training methods face a trade-off: tight relaxation bounds sacrifice standard accuracy, while adversarial training yields empirically robust but hard-to-certify models. The goal is to achieve better standard-certified accuracy trade-offs.

Method: AD-CERT introduces a certified training objective that combines adversarial distillation with an IBP upper bound. It distills adversarial information over the logit space from an empirically robust teacher, serving as an effective lower bound surrogate for certified training.

Result: AD-CERT achieves state-of-the-art certified performance on several robustness benchmarks. Logit-level distillation improves certified accuracy over feature-space distillation by up to 5.40 percentage points.

Conclusion: Distilling adversarial information at the logit level from an empirically robust teacher provides an effective lower bound for certified training, enabling better certified robustness without sacrificing standard accuracy as much.

Abstract: Certified training aims to produce models whose predictions can be formally verified against adversarial perturbations, typically by optimising upper bounds on the worst-case loss over an allowed perturbation set. For neural networks, certified training methods based purely on tight relaxation bounds produce networks that are amenable to certification, but sacrifice standard accuracy. Conversely, adversarial training often yields stronger empirical robustness and standard accuracy, but the resulting models are generally difficult to certify with neural network verifiers. Recently, the literature has shown that better standard-certified accuracy trade-offs can be achieved by combining adversarial training objectives with loose over-approximations based on Interval Bound Propagation (IBP), effectively interpolating between lower and upper bounds of the worst-case loss. Building on this, we introduce AD-CERT, a certified training objective that combines adversarial distillation with an IBP upper bound. We show that distilling adversarial information over the logit space from an empirically robust teacher provides an effective lower bound surrogate for certified training, with AD-CERT achieving state-of-the-art certified performance on several robustness benchmarks. Furthermore, in a unified setup, distilling adversarial information at the logit-level is shown to improve certified accuracy over a robust feature-space distillation objective by up to 5.40 percentage points.

</details>


### [218] [When to Truncate a Feature Ranking: A Residual-Overlap Stopping Rule for Subset Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31686)
*Jesus S. Aguilar-Ruiz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a distributional framework to transform supervised feature rankings into class-independent subsets using a risk-calibrated stopping rule based on the Bhattacharyya coefficient, reducing tens of thousands of features to a few dozen while maintaining predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature ranking methods lack a principled way to determine how many top-ranked features to retain; the cardinality is often chosen arbitrarily or via cross-validation without direct interpretation. The paper addresses the question: when is there enough accumulated class-separation evidence to stop selecting features?

Method: The method uses the Bhattacharyya coefficient to measure marginal separation between class-conditional distributions for each variable. It selects the shortest prefix of a ranking where the residual product overlap falls below a prescribed threshold for all relevant class contrasts. Binary and multiclass Bayes-risk bounds are derived for calibrating the threshold from a target all-pairs risk level.

Result: Empirical evaluation on high-dimensional genomic datasets shows the method reduces tens of thousands of variables to a few dozen while maintaining predictive performance statistically comparable to the all-features baseline.

Conclusion: The stopping rule is well-suited for very high-dimensional settings where exhaustive subset search is infeasible and interpretable truncation of feature rankings is essential, as it only requires one-dimensional marginal overlap estimates and scans a precomputed ranking.

Abstract: Feature rankings are widely used in supervised feature selection because they are simple, scalable and easy to interpret. Variables are first ranked by a relevance score, and a subset is then obtained by retaining the top-ranked variables. Although the first stage has been extensively studied, the second is often governed by an arbitrary cardinality, an empirical threshold or cross-validation, without a direct interpretation. This raises a basic question: given a feature ranking, when is there enough accumulated class-separation evidence to stop selecting features?
  This paper develops a distributional framework for transforming supervised feature rankings into class-independent subsets through an explicit risk-calibrated stopping rule. For each variable and each pair of classes, marginal separation is measured by the Bhattacharyya coefficient between the corresponding class-conditional distributions. The proposed method selects a single global subset shared by all classes by retaining the shortest prefix of a ranking whose residual product overlap falls below a prescribed threshold for every relevant class contrast. We derive binary and multiclass Bayes-risk bounds for the labelled product marginal problem, and obtain prior-dependent and prior-free calibrations of the residual-overlap threshold from a target all-pairs risk level.
  An empirical comparison on high-dimensional genomic datasets illustrates that the rule can reduce tens of thousands of variables to a few dozen while maintaining predictive performance statistically comparable to the all-features baseline. As the stopping rule only requires one-dimensional marginal overlap estimates and scans a precomputed ranking, it is well suited to very high-dimensional settings where exhaustive subset search is infeasible and interpretable truncation of feature rankings is essential.

</details>


### [219] [Diffusing Blame: Task-Dependent Credit Assignment in Biologically Plausible Dual-Stream Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31700)
*Yutaro Yamada,Luca Grillotti,Rujikorn Charakorn,Sebastian Risi,David Ha,Robert Tjarko Lange*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends Error Diffusion (ED) to work under Dale's principle (neurons are either excitatory or inhibitory) for both supervised classification and reinforcement learning. The authors introduce modulo error routing, achieve 96.7% on MNIST and 61.7% on CIFAR-10, and propose three domain-specific innovations. They also integrate ED with PPO for continuous control tasks, achieving competitive results against Direct Feedback Alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To scale biologically plausible learning rules under Dale's principle beyond simple MNIST classification, covering both supervised and reinforcement learning settings, and to understand task-dependent credit-assignment bottlenecks.

Method: Modulo error routing extends Error Diffusion; dual-stream excitatory/inhibitory architecture; three innovations: layer-specific sigmoid widths, batch-centered class error signals, asymmetric initialization; ED-PPO integration for RL.

Result: 96.7% on MNIST, 61.7% on CIFAR-10; competitive RL performance with ED-PPO vs. Direct Feedback Alignment on Brax and Craftax tasks.

Conclusion: Representation learning is feasible under strict Dale's principle, and the proposed method scales to both classification and RL problems beyond simple benchmarks.

Abstract: Biological neural circuits obey Dale's principle: each neuron's synapses are uniformly excitatory or inhibitory. Artificial networks that respect this constraint must coordinate separate excitatory and inhibitory populations, fundamentally changing how credit is assigned during learning. Several biologically plausible learning rules avoid backpropagation's weight transport requirement, but it has been difficult to achieve strong performance under Dale's principle beyond MNIST. Error Diffusion (ED) was originally proposed in a dual-stream excitatory/inhibitory architecture, where learning is driven by routing global error signals to all layers without transporting transposed forward weights or relying on random feedback matrices. Whether such a rule can scale under Dale's principle across both supervised classification and reinforcement learning remains unknown. Here, we introduce modulo error routing to extend Error Diffusion beyond binary classification, and show that a dual-stream excitatory/inhibitory architecture trained with this method achieves 96.7% on MNIST and establishes a 61.7% baseline on CIFAR-10, demonstrating that representation learning is possible even when strictly enforcing Dale's principle. For the classification setting, we introduce three domain-specific innovations: layer-specific sigmoid widths, batch-centered class error signals, and asymmetric initialization, and ablation analysis reveals that their relative importance reverses between MNIST and CIFAR-10, exposing task-dependent credit-assignment bottlenecks invisible to single-benchmark evaluation. In reinforcement learning, we integrate ED with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and evaluate it on continuous-control tasks in Google Brax and on Craftax, an open-ended exploration task. We show that ED-PPO achieves competitive performance relative to Direct Feedback Alignment, a backpropagation-free baseline.

</details>


### [220] [Nonlinearity-Aware LoRA: Structured Gate Adaptation under Low-Rank Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31717)
*Shuai Yuan,Sudong Cai,Bingzhi Chen,Shuyuan Zheng,Chuan Xiao,Makoto Onizuka,Rui Mao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NA-LoRA improves parameter-efficient fine-tuning by addressing selection misalignment in gated FFNs through derivative-based temporal-importance masking and activation-specific step-scaling, without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LoRA view as update-space approximation is incomplete for self-gated Transformer FFNs because low-rank updates can misalign selection weights. The paper formalizes "selection misalignment" and connects it to local effective homogeneity of activations, motivating a nonlinearity-aware principle for PEFT.

Method: NA-LoRA: (1) derivative-based temporal-importance mask for gate-related LoRA updates, (2) activation-specific step-scaling rule when a coarse effective-homogeneity partition is available. Training-only, no auxiliary loss, no inference overhead.

Result: Consistently improves over vanilla LoRA on language-model fine-tuning and vision-language transfer benchmarks; competitive with or better than strong PEFT variants.

Conclusion: Nonlinearity-aware fine-tuning (NA-LoRA) effectively addresses the selection misalignment problem in gated FFNs, offering a principled and lightweight improvement over vanilla LoRA.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is commonly viewed as an update-space approximation to full fine-tuning, yet this view is incomplete for self-gated Transformer feed-forward networks. In gated FFNs, a low-rank residual can change not only projected features but also the nonlinear selection weights that determine which channels contribute to the output. We formalize this effect as selection misalignment and connect it to the local effective homogeneity of self-gated activations. This motivates a nonlinearity-aware principle for parameter-efficient fine-tuning: low-rank updates should allocate capacity to gate channels whose nonlinear states remain responsive and should shape the temporal evolution of selection. We propose NA-LoRA, a training-only method with two lightweight mechanisms: a derivative-based temporal-importance mask for gate-related LoRA updates and an activation-specific step-scaling rule when a meaningful coarse effective-homogeneity partition is available. NA-LoRA adds no auxiliary loss and incurs no inference-time overhead. Experiments on language-model fine-tuning and vision-language transfer benchmarks show that NA-LoRA consistently improves over vanilla LoRA and is competitive with or better than strong PEFT variants.

</details>


### [221] [FedXDS: Leveraging Model Attribution Methods to counteract Data Heterogeneity in Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31742)
*Maximilian Andreas Hoefler,Karsten Mueller,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedXDS is the first method using XAI-based feature attribution for selective data sharing in federated learning, achieving higher accuracy and faster convergence under heterogeneous data distributions with formal privacy guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While XAI has been used for model transparency, its potential in federated learning remains unexplored. Federated learning suffers from performance degradation due to statistical heterogeneity (non-IID data) across clients, and existing methods lack fine-grained guidance on what data to share.

Method: FedXDS employs propagation-based attribution (XAI) to identify task-relevant features via a single backward pass, enabling selective data sharing between clients. It incorporates metric privacy techniques to provide formal privacy guarantees while preserving utility.

Result: Consistently achieves higher accuracy and faster convergence than existing methods across varying client numbers and heterogeneity settings. Empirically robust against membership inference and feature inversion attacks.

Conclusion: XAI-guided selective data sharing effectively mitigates statistical heterogeneity in federated learning, with theoretical privacy guarantees and practical robustness. Code is open-sourced.

Abstract: Explainable AI (XAI) methods have demonstrated significant success in recent years at identifying relevant features in input data that drive deep learning model decisions, enhancing interpretability for users. However, the potential of XAI beyond providing model transparency has remained largely unexplored in adjacent machine learning domains. In this paper, we show for the first time how XAI can be utilized in the context of federated learning. Specifically, while federated learning enables collaborative model training without raw data sharing, it suffers from performance degradation when client data distributions exhibit statistical heterogeneity. We introduce FedXDS (Federated Learning via XAI-guided Data Sharing), the first approach to utilize feature attribution techniques to identify precisely which data elements should be selectively shared between clients to mitigate heterogeneity. By employing propagation-based attribution, our method identifies task-relevant features through a single backward pass, enabling selective data sharing that aligns client contributions. To protect sensitive information, we incorporate metric privacy techniques that provide formal privacy guarantees while preserving utility. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach consistently achieves higher accuracy and faster convergence compared to existing methods across varying client numbers and heterogeneity settings. We provide theoretical privacy guarantees and empirically demonstrate robustness against both membership inference and feature inversion attacks. Code is available at https://github.com/MaxH1996/FedXDS.

</details>


### [222] [Addressing Over-Refusal in LLMs with Competing Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31748)
*Taeyoun Kim,Aviral Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SEAR trains language models to explore harmful reasoning as a signal to better distinguish harmful from harmless prompts, then flip to safe answers, mitigating over-refusal without sacrificing safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety training causes over-refusal on harmless prompts; RL-based reasoning still suffers from "rubber stamp" predetermined responses. The paper aims to resolve the safety-refusal trade-off more fundamentally.

Method: Adversarial optimization with two roles: a reasoning player explores unsafe reasoning strategies, and an answer player ensures final safe output. A single model plays both roles via chain-of-thought with dense process rewards for stable optimization of competing objectives.

Result: SEAR mitigates over-refusal on harmless prompts while maintaining safety on harmful ones, and defends against attacks that manipulate reasoning toward harmful outputs.

Conclusion: Deliberate exploration of unsafe reasoning, rather than suppression, improves safety alignment by resolving ambiguity, enabling both compliance and refusal when appropriate.

Abstract: Safety training on language models often induces over-refusal: improved safety on harmful prompts at the cost of increased refusal on harmless ones. Though this trade-off can be mitigated by training models with reinforcement learning (RL) to reason before answering, it does not remove the underlying problem that reasoning can often be a "rubber stamp" for a predetermined response. In this paper, we address the safety-refusal trade-off by rethinking how models are trained to reason about safety. Our key insight is that unsafe reasoning can itself serve as a useful exploratory signal. Rather than preemptively blocking harmful thoughts, we encourage the model to sufficiently explore unsafe reasoning but produce a safe response. The harmful exploration improves the model's ability to distinguish harmful from harmless prompts by resolving ambiguity, allowing it to remain safe while complying only when appropriate. We cast this as an adversarial optimization problem in which a reasoning player explores strategies for producing an unsafe response and an answer player ensures that the final output is safe. We train a single model with dense rewards to play both roles within one chain-of-thought, across different segments. To achieve this, we find that process rewards are crucial for stable optimization of competing objectives. Our resulting model SEAR deliberately engages in harmful reasoning as exploration while reliably flipping back to a safe answer. We demonstrate that this behavior helps mitigate over-refusal and defend against attacks that directly manipulate the reasoning to be harmful.

</details>


### [223] [Policy Optimization Achieves Data-Dependent Regret Bounds in MDPs with Unknown Transitions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31769)
*Mingyi Li,Taira Tsuchiya,Kenji Yamanishi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a policy optimization algorithm for online episodic tabular MDPs with unknown transitions that achieves data-dependent regret bounds (first-order, second-order, path-length) and best-of-both-worlds guarantees, resolving an open problem left by prior work that assumed known transitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work achieved data-dependent regret bounds for policy optimization under adversarial and stochastic losses, but only with known transition kernels. The open question was whether such guarantees are possible when the transition kernel is unknown.

Method: The authors develop an optimistic follow-the-regularized-leader algorithm featuring new optimistic Q-function estimators and a data-dependent transition bonus that controls estimator bias via loss-prediction error.

Result: The algorithm achieves first-order, second-order, and path-length regret bounds with an unavoidable transition-dependent complexity term, along with gap-dependent polylog(T) regret in the stochastic regime.

Conclusion: Data-dependent best-of-both-worlds guarantees are achievable under unknown transitions via policy optimization, though an intrinsic transition-dependent term emerges as the cost of learning the unknown kernel.

Abstract: We study policy optimization for online episodic tabular Markov decision processes with unknown transition kernels, aiming for best-of-both-worlds guarantees together with data-dependent regret bounds. Recent work (Dann et al., 2023; Li et al., 2026) has shown that policy optimization can adapt to both adversarial and stochastic losses with first-order, second-order, and path-length bounds, but only under known transitions, leaving open whether such data-dependent guarantees are achievable by policy optimization when the transition kernel is unknown. We resolve this by developing a new algorithm based on optimistic follow-the-regularized-leader that attains these guarantees under unknown transitions. The key ingredient is a new design of optimistic $Q$-function estimators together with a data-dependent transition bonus that controls estimator bias through the loss-prediction error. Our analysis further identifies an unavoidable transition-dependent complexity term that captures the intrinsic cost of estimating the transition kernel. As a result, we obtain first-order, second-order, and path-length bounds with the transition-dependent complexity term while simultaneously achieving gap-dependent $\mathrm{polylog}(T)$ regret in the stochastic regime.

</details>


### [224] [Bridging the Gap Between Latent and Explicit Reasoning with Looped Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31779)
*Ying Fan,Anej Svete,Kangwook Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LOTUS uses looped Transformers with parallel supervision on latent representations to achieve explicit-CoT-level performance at 3B scale with 2.5x-6.9x latency reduction, demonstrating interpretable latent reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing latent CoT methods underperform explicit CoT beyond 1B parameters, with the gap widening at scale. Looped Transformers are a natural fit for latent reasoning but haven't been shown to bridge this gap.

Method: LOTUS uses a looped padded Transformer that processes K latent blocks in parallel for R iterations, applying cross-entropy loss on each latent position's gold CoT-step token for supervision.

Result: LOTUS is the first latent-CoT method to match explicit CoT at 3B scale, achieving 2.5x-6.9x latency reduction. Its latent space is interpretable and CoT-aligned, recovering gold reasoning steps and surfacing alternative valid intermediate steps.

Conclusion: Both the looped backbone and parallel supervision on gold CoT tokens are essential for bridging the gap between latent and explicit CoT reasoning.

Abstract: Language models typically reason via explicit chain-of-thought (CoT), generating intermediate steps token-by-token. Latent CoT offers an alternative: it performs multi-step reasoning in the model's hidden states, replacing decoded tokens with continuous representations for greater efficiency. However, existing latent CoT methods underperform explicit CoT beyond 1B parameters, and the gap widens with scale. Looped, or recurrent-depth, Transformers, which reuse their weights to increase computation depth without adding parameters, are a natural fit for latent reasoning. We therefore ask whether looped Transformers can bridge this gap. We answer affirmatively with a simple recipe: a looped padded Transformer that processes K latent blocks in parallel for R iterations, with a cross-entropy loss on each latent position's gold CoT-step token, similar to explicit CoT supervision. We instantiate it as LOTUS (Looped Transformers with parallel supervision on latents). LOTUS is, to our knowledge, the first latent-CoT method to bridge the gap to explicit CoT at the 3B scale, while cutting thought-phase latency by 2.5x-6.9x from compact math expressions to natural language. Projecting LOTUS's post-loop latents through the base LM head recovers the gold reasoning steps and even surfaces alternative valid intermediate steps, evidence that its latent space is interpretable and CoT-aligned. Ablations confirm that both the looped backbone and the parallel supervision on gold CoT tokens are essential.

</details>


### [225] [Relational and Sequential Conformal Inference for Energy Time Series over Graphs via Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31804)
*Keivan Faghih Niresi,Alice Cicirello,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STOIC integrates spatial-temporal graph neural networks with tabular foundation models using conformal prediction to provide calibrated uncertainty estimates for energy demand forecasting, outperforming baselines across five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Point forecasts alone are insufficient for real-world energy systems; operators need reliable uncertainty estimates for risk-aware decision-making, grid stability, and operational planning under uncertainty.

Method: STOIC first generates point forecasts via STGNN, then reformulates spatial-temporal residuals into a tabular representation, and uses a tabular foundation model for zero-shot calibration of prediction intervals via in-context learning.

Result: STOIC consistently outperforms existing conformal prediction baselines across synthetic simulations, real-world electricity, and district heating network benchmarks.

Conclusion: STOIC delivers more reliable and robust uncertainty estimates for complex graph-structured energy time series without task-specific retraining.

Abstract: Accurate energy demand forecasting is essential for the reliable operation and planning of modern sustainable energy systems. Spatial-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) have recently achieved strong performance in point forecasting by jointly modeling temporal dynamics and relational dependencies across interconnected energy nodes. However, in real-world energy systems, accurate point forecasts alone are insufficient, as operators also require reliable uncertainty estimates to support risk-aware decision-making, grid stability, and operational planning under uncertainty. Conformal prediction provides a principled and model-agnostic framework for uncertainty quantification with statistical coverage guarantees, making it particularly attractive for safety-critical energy applications. However, existing conformal prediction approaches often fail to fully capture the complex spatial-temporal structure of energy systems. To address these limitations, we propose STOIC (Spatial-Temporal Graph Conformal Prediction with In-Context Learning), a novel framework that integrates graph-based forecasting with the zero-shot calibration capabilities of tabular foundation models. STOIC first generates point forecasts using an STGNN and subsequently reformulates spatial-temporal residuals into a tabular representation suitable for in-context learning. Leveraging a tabular foundation model, STOIC calibrates prediction intervals without task-specific retraining, effectively capturing both sequential and relational dependencies. We evaluate STOIC on five diverse benchmarks, including synthetic simulations as well as real-world electricity and district heating networks. Across all datasets, STOIC consistently outperforms existing conformal prediction baselines, delivering more reliable and robust uncertainty estimates for complex graph-structured energy time series.

</details>


### [226] [Geometry-Preserving Orthonormal Initialization for Low-Rank Adaptation in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31813)
*Ruijia Zhang,Jiacheng Zhu,Hanqing Zhu,Laixi Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes LoRA initialization in RLVR, proposes geometry-preserving orthonormal initialization (RLPO/RLMO) that stabilizes training and outperforms standard LoRA, and explains why PiSSA/MiLoRA underperform in RLVR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA variants like PiSSA and MiLoRA work well under SFT but underperform and show instability under RLVR, revealing that how to initialize low-rank matrices in RLVR is poorly understood.

Method: Develops theoretical analysis showing orthonormal initialization minimizes the gap between LoRA and full fine-tuning in RLVR; proposes geometry-preserving orthonormal initialization leading to two new variants: RLPO and RLMO.

Result: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks, the proposed orthonormal initialization stabilizes RLVR training, outperforms standard LoRA, and contrasts with the underperformance of PiSSA and MiLoRA.

Conclusion: Orthonormal initialization is key to effective LoRA in RLVR; the unified analysis explains failures of prior variants and offers insights of independent interest.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) and its variants enable parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models under the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) paradigm. However, their efficacy and behavior under Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) are less well understood. In particular, two structurally initialized LoRA variants, PiSSA and MiLoRA, which outperform standard LoRA under SFT, can underperform standard LoRA under RLVR and may even exhibit training instability. These observations suggest that how to initialize the low-rank matrices in RLVR remains unclear. In this work, we develop a theoretical analysis of LoRA in RLVR, showing that orthonormal initialization achieves the minimal gap between LoRA outcome and that of full fine-tuning. Guided by this insight, we propose geometry-preserving orthonormal initialization for low-rank adaptation in RLVR, leading to two new variants, RLPO and RLMO. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that the proposed orthonormal initialization stabilizes RLVR training and outperforms standard LoRA, contrasting with PiSSA and MiLoRA. Finally, our unified analysis for LoRA initialization also explains why PiSSA and MiLoRA can underperform in RLVR, which may be of independent interest. Code and checkpoints are publicly available at https://github.com/Richard-ZZZ/geometry-preserving-orthonormal-init-rlvr.

</details>


### [227] [Low-dimensional topology of deep neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31856)
*Junyu Ren,Lek-Heng Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies how neural networks with layer width restricted to d=3 (ℝ³ as representation space) change topological invariants like linking number, comparing feedforward networks, ResNets, and transformers; finding that ResNets and transformers have equal power, both surpass feedforward networks with monotonic activations, and nonmonotonic activations elevate feedforward networks to equal ResNets/transformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how neural network architectures affect topological structures by restricting to ℝ³ (where topological invariants are nontrivial and visualizable), rather than using high dimensions where topology trivializes, providing a controlled framework to compare architectural expressivity.

Method: Restricts each layer to width d=3 (ℝ³), uses linking number as the primary topological invariant, provides full proofs and empirical experiments comparing feedforward networks, ResNets, and transformers in their ability to change linking numbers through layers. Also generalizes results to arbitrary d>3.

Result: Layer-skipping in ResNets equals attention in transformers in power to change linking numbers; both are strictly more powerful than feedforward networks with monotonic activations; feedforward with monotonic activations beats invertible/flow models; nonmonotonic activation makes feedforward equal to ResNets/transformers.

Conclusion: Low-dimensional topology can be a useful tool to guide designs of AI architectures; the findings establish a topological hierarchy of neural network expressivity classes.

Abstract: We study layered models, including feedforward networks, ResNets, and transformers, by limiting each layer to a width of $d = 3$, i.e., $\mathbb{R}^3$ as representation space. This allows us to track how a neural network changes low-dimensional topological invariants through its layers. Just about any topological structure may be simplified or even trivialized by simply increasing dimension; e.g., any knot is equivalent to an unknot in $\mathbb{R}^4$. By restricting to $\mathbb{R}^3$, we not only isolate the effects of activation and depth from that of width, we work in a space that lends itself to easy visualization. We focus on linking number here, deferring other invariants like link groups, Milnor's $\barμ$-invariants, knot types, ambient cobordisms, to a sequel. We provide full proofs and empirical experiments to justify the following insights: When measured by their power to effect changes in linking numbers, the layer-skipping feature in ResNets is as powerful as the attention mechanism in transformers; both ResNets and transformers are strictly more powerful than feedforward neural networks with monotonic activations, which are in turn more powerful than invertible and flow-based models; but replacing monotonic activation with a nonmonotonic one elevates a feedforward network into the same expressivity class as ResNets and transformers. These results suggest that low-dimensional topology can be a useful tool to guide designs of AI architectures. We also generalize our results from $d = 3$ to arbitrary $d > 3$.

</details>


### [228] [Review Residuals: Update-Conditioned Residual Gating for Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31859)
*Kyle Kramer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Review Residuals introduce learned, input-dependent gating for residual connections, conditioning on both state and update. They show depth stability and emergence-of-benefit at scale (≥590M parameters), outperforming standard residuals and Highway gates at larger model sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard residual connections blindly add sublayer updates with a fixed coefficient of one, never verifying reliability before committing. This work draws on the human-factors principle of independent verification to make residual connections more adaptive.

Method: Introduces Review Residuals: h_l = h_{l-1} + r_l * u_l where r_l = sigmoid(W[RMSNorm(h_{l-1}), RMSNorm(u_l)]). The gate conditions on both current state and proposed update, distinguishing it from prior gated/scaled residuals.

Result: (1) Depth stability: additive identity-preserving form trains stably at all depths, while convex (Highway-style) gating reintroduces vanishing gradients beyond ~20 layers. (2) Emergence with scale: no advantage at small scale (60M-1B params), but at 590M and 1B significantly outperforms Highway gates and standard residuals (p<0.05), with benefit growing rather than shrinking with model size.

Conclusion: Review Residuals provide a depth-stable alternative to standard residuals that yields growing benefits at larger model scales, suggesting adaptive gating conditioned on both state and update becomes increasingly valuable as models grow.

Abstract: Residual connections add every sublayer's proposed update with a fixed coefficient of one; the network never evaluates whether an update is reliable before committing it. Drawing on the human-factors principle of independent verification, we introduce Review Residuals, which scale each update by a learned, input-dependent gate conditioned on both the current state and the proposed update: h_l = h_{l-1} + r_l * u_l with r_l = sigmoid(W[RMSNorm(h_{l-1}), RMSNorm(u_l)]). Conditioning the gate on the update is the property that distinguishes it from prior gated and scaled residuals. We report two findings. First, a depth-stability result: a convex (Highway-style) form of the gate reintroduces vanishing gradients and fails to train beyond ~20 layers, whereas the additive, identity-preserving form trains stably at all depths we tested. Second, an emergence-with-scale result: trained from scratch across five sizes (60M-1B parameters, multi-seed), Review Residuals show no advantage at small scale but at 590M significantly outperform both a parameter-matched Highway gate and a parameter-matched standard residual (p<0.05), with a larger advantage at 1B. The benefit grows with model size rather than shrinking.

</details>


### [229] [Sequential RC-TGAN: Generating Relational Time Series with Spectral Envelope Loss](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31904)
*Mohamed Gueye,Yazid Attabi,Manuel Morales,Maxime Dumas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Seq. RC-TGAN extends RC-TGAN with Spectral Envelope Theory loss to preserve periodic structures in synthetic relational databases, using VGM discretization for continuous data, and introduces two new spectral evaluation metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Handling categorical time series (e.g., status codes) in synthetic relational databases is challenging because standard encoding methods like one-hot encoding fail to capture frequency-domain features such as seasonality and cyclicity.

Method: Seq. RC-TGAN integrates a differentiable Spectral Envelope loss for categorical sequences, extends it to continuous data via VGM discretization, and proposes Spectral Density Divergence & Spectral Envelope Divergence metrics.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art systems in reproducing cyclic patterns and long-term seasonality on both real-world datasets and simulated benchmarks with known theoretical spectral envelopes.

Conclusion: End-to-end frequency-domain regularization significantly improves fidelity of periodic structures in synthetic relational time series for both categorical and continuous features.

Abstract: The generation of synthetic relational databases often involves modeling complex temporal dynamics, such as transaction logs or event sequences. A significant challenge in this domain is the handling of categorical time series (e.g., status codes), where standard encoding methods like one-hot encoding fail to capture intrinsic frequency-domain features such as seasonality and cyclicity. In this paper, we introduce Sequential RC-TGAN (Seq. RC-TGAN), a temporal extension of the RC-TGAN framework, equipped with a novel integrated loss function based on the \textit{Spectral Envelope Theory}. This differentiable loss allows the generator to directly optimize the preservation of latent periodic structures via backpropagation. While spectral envelope theory is inherently designed for categorical sequences, we extend this frequency-domain regularization to continuous time series by employing a Variational Gaussian Mixture Model (VGM) discretization strategy. To establish a mathematically rigorous evaluation standard, we simulate categorical time series governed by a parameter $α$, with exactly known theoretical spectral envelopes. Integrating these dynamic sequences into the child tables of a relational database yields a robust ground-truth benchmark for evaluating the frequency-domain fidelity of our generative framework. Furthermore, we address the lack of robust evaluation standards for relational time series by proposing two new metrics: Spectral Density Divergence and Spectral Envelope Divergence. Experimental results on real-world datasets, as well as our simulated benchmarks, demonstrate that our end-to-end approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art systems in reproducing cyclic patterns and long-term seasonality across both categorical and continuous features.

</details>


### [230] [Interface-Aware Neural Newton Preconditioning for Robust Cohesive Zone Model Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31921)
*Zhangyong Liang,Huanhuan Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an Interface-Aware Neural Newton Preconditioner (IA-NNP) that uses learned, state-dependent interface corrections to improve convergence of Cohesive Zone Models in implicit quasi-static finite element analyses, outperforming standard Newton-Raphson and manual modifications on benchmark tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cohesive Zone Models (CZMs) in implicit quasi-static FEA often suffer from convergence issues (stagnation, wrong-branch convergence, step cuts) due to softening-induced negative tangents, solution jumps, and Newton-basin mismatch. Existing remedies like viscous regularization, path following, or manual NR modification alter the effective response, increase computational cost, or rely on hand-crafted rules.

Method: IA-NNP is proposed with two realizations: IA-NNP-Init (learned initial-guess lifting) and IA-NNP-NL (iteration-level nonlinear right preconditioning). It recasts manual NR modification as rule-based interface lifting and generalizes it into a learned correction using interface graph features (opening, traction, tangent, damage/history, mode mixity, residuals, neighboring states). Corrections are bounded, confidence-gated, and only accepted through the original CZM Newton solve, preserving the original traction-separation law.

Result: Tests on horizontal, circular, two-interface, and active-front benchmarks show improved difficult-increment convergence, better branch recovery, and fewer failures than standard NR and manual NR modification, while preserving the force-displacement response.

Conclusion: IA-NNP effectively improves CZM convergence without altering the underlying discrete solution, as shown by a root-equivalence property. It offers a learned, automated alternative to hand-crafted interface rules.

Abstract: Cohesive Zone Models (CZMs) are widely used to simulate interface fracture, delamination, adhesive failure, and fiber--matrix debonding in aerospace composite structures. In implicit quasi-static finite element analyses, cohesive softening may introduce negative interface tangents, solution jumps, and Newton-basin mismatch, so the previous converged state can become a poor initial guess for the next increment. This may lead to stagnation, wrong-branch convergence, or repeated step cuts. Existing remedies, including viscous regularization, path following, dynamic relaxation, and manual Newton--Raphson (NR) modification, either alter the effective response, increase cost, or rely on hand-crafted interface rules. This work proposes an Interface-Aware Neural Newton Preconditioner (IA-NNP) for difficult CZM increments. IA-NNP recasts manual NR modification as rule-based interface lifting and generalizes it into a learned, state-dependent interface correction. The method acts only on active interface variables and preserves the original traction--separation law, residual assembly, tangent evaluation, history update, and dissipation checks. Two realizations are developed: IA-NNP-Init for learned initial-guess lifting and IA-NNP-NL for iteration-level nonlinear right preconditioning. Interface graph features encode opening, traction, tangent, damage/history variables, mode mixity, residuals, and neighboring states. The correction is bounded, confidence-gated, and accepted only through the original CZM Newton solve. A root-equivalence property shows that IA-NNP changes the path to convergence but not the discrete CZM solution set. Tests on horizontal, circular, two-interface, and active-front benchmarks show improved difficult-increment convergence, better branch recovery, and fewer failures than standard NR and manual NR modification, while preserving the force--displacement response.

</details>


### [231] [Making Sense of Touch from the Child's View for Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31943)
*Max Whitton,Zecheng Wang,Puchen Liu,Quang Tuan Truong,Shengao Wang,Manaswi Yadamreddy,Oktay Ozel,Visista Jayanti,Saniya Sekhon,Hanna Samuel Tadesse,Lawrence Miao,Junjie Wang,Jiasen Lu,Chen Yu,Boqing Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether touch helps babies learn visual concepts, introduces a coding system for baby touch events, and uses a dataset of 264k touch clips to pretrain developmentally grounded models for insights into early learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify the importance of touch in human babies' visual concept learning and understand how much babies rely on tactile experiences for visual development.

Method: Proposes a structured coding system for baby-centric touch events, constructs a dataset of 264k two-second touch clips, and uses it to pretrain developmentally grounded models.

Result: A dataset of 264k two-second clips of touch events was created, and developmentally grounded models were pretrained, yielding promising insights into baby learning from touch.

Conclusion: Touch appears to play a meaningful role in visual concept learning during infancy, and the proposed coding system and dataset provide a principled way to study this phenomenon.

Abstract: Is the sense of touch a mechanism for human babies' learning of visual concepts? If so, can we quantify its importance, and to what extent do babies rely on their sense of touch for visual learning? To approach these questions in a principled way, we propose a structured coding system for baby-centric touch events, yielding a dataset of 264k two-second clips of touch events coded according to this system. Using this dataset, we pretrain developmentally grounded models that reveal promising insights into the nature of baby learning from touch.

</details>


### [232] [Signed-Permutation Coordinate Transport for RMSNorm Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31963)
*John Sweeney*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that coordinate-indexed operations in LLMs (steering vectors, SAEs, merges) require fixing the model's residual-stream gauge, which is architecture-dependent: LayerNorm has permutation gauge S_d, while RMSNorm has signed-permutation gauge B_d = S_d × {±1}^d. The authors introduce sign-marginalized Hungarian matching to handle the B_d gauge, demonstrate that permutation-only alignment fails for RMSNorm models, and show that coordinate-preserving transport along fine-tuning trajectories recovers 91.1% of cross-run coordinates. They also show that proper gauge handling preserves tool transfer, stateful training, and interpretability reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern LLM workflows involve moving coordinate-indexed objects across checkpoints (steering vectors, SAEs, neuron sets, attribution lists, merge alignments), but this is only well-posed after fixing the model's residual-stream gauge. The authors identify that the gauge is architecture-dependent and that permutation-only alignment is symmetry-incomplete for RMSNorm models, motivating the need for proper gauge handling.

Method: The authors identify the gauge structure of LayerNorm (permutation gauge S_d) and RMSNorm (signed-permutation gauge B_d). They introduce sign-marginalized Hungarian matching to handle the B_d gauge. They propose coordinate-preserving transport (rather than function-level merging) as the primary object, composing saved-checkpoint local B_d gauges along same-base fine-tuning trajectories.

Result: Coordinate-preserving transport along fine-tuning trajectories recovers 91.1% of cross-run coordinates at 1500 steps versus 60.3% for endpoint matching. TinyLlama SAE reconstruction achieves NMSE 0.004 under B_d versus 1.08 under S_d. Qwen sentiment steering preserves 95.8% of its effect versus 17.2%. Refusal steering reverses sign under S_d. Signed transport of AdamW state preserves the resumed trajectory while permutation-only state follows a different one.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that index-level interpretability claims are reproducible only relative to an explicit gauge. The proper handling of signed-permutation gauge B_d is essential for RMSNorm models, and permutation-only alignment is fundamentally incomplete for such architectures. Coordinate-preserving transport significantly outperforms endpoint matching for cross-run coordination.

Abstract: Modern LLM workflows move coordinate-indexed objects across checkpoints: steering vectors, sparse autoencoders, top-$k$ neuron sets, attribution lists, and merge alignments. This is only well posed after fixing the model's residual-stream gauge, which we show is architecture-dependent: LayerNorm residual charts have permutation gauge $S_d$ (up to a global sign flip), while RMSNorm charts with generic per-channel gain have signed-permutation gauge $B_d = S_d \ltimes \{\pm 1\}^d$. Permutation-only alignment is therefore symmetry-incomplete for RMSNorm models. We introduce sign-marginalized Hungarian matching and prove a sharp failure mode: with decorrelated coordinates, raw signed-correlation matching has a structural permutation-accuracy ceiling at the positive-sign fraction of the true gauge, which sign-marginalization removes. We then make coordinate-preserving transport, not function-level merging, the primary object: composing saved-checkpoint local $B_d$ gauges along same-base fine-tuning trajectories recovers 91.1% of cross-run coordinates at 1500 steps versus 60.3% for endpoint matching, and the gain is not explained by merely routing through the base. The recovered gauge transfers tools that permutation-only alignment breaks: TinyLlama SAE reconstruction has NMSE 0.004 under $B_d$ versus 1.08 under $S_d$; Qwen sentiment steering preserves 95.8% of its effect versus 17.2%; refusal steering reverses sign under $S_d$; coordinate-preserving merges behave the same way. The same covariance governs stateful training: signed transport of AdamW state preserves the resumed trajectory, while permutation-only state follows a different one from a functionally identical checkpoint. Finally, gauge-sweep audits show index-level interpretability claims are reproducible only relative to an explicit gauge.

</details>


### [233] [Amplifying Membership Signal Through Chained Regeneration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31991)
*Wojciech Łapacz,Stanisław Pawlak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MADreMIA is a framework that enhances membership and dataset inference attacks across generative models by using iterative chained generations to detect memorized training samples, which show higher coherence and slower degradation than non-members.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large generative models tend to memorize training data, making sample verification critical for privacy auditing and copyright enforcement. Existing MIA and DI attacks rely on one-shot generations that yield weak signals and limited sensitivity across modalities.

Method: The framework uses chained generations across diverse modalities, where each output serves as the subsequent input (iterative trajectories), leveraging inherent signals of memorized samples (higher coherence and slower degradation) without shadow model training.

Result: MADreMIA provides richer membership evidence at low FPR across diverse model families and modalities, demonstrated for IARs, diffusion models, language models, and preliminary results for audio models.

Conclusion: Iterative regeneration trajectories effectively amplify membership signals in generative models, enabling scalable model-agnostic inference without costly shadow model training.

Abstract: The tendency of large generative models to memorize training data makes sample verification critical for privacy auditing and copyright enforcement. Current membership (MIA) and dataset inference (DI) attacks often rely on one-shot generations, which yield weak signals and limited sensitivity across modalities. Inspired by Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD), we introduce MADreMIA, a model-agnostic framework that enhances white-, gray-, and black-box MIA and DI. Rather than relying on shadow model training -- often infeasible for large generative models -- our framework facilitates scalable inference by leveraging inherent signals through iterative trajectories. This process utilizes chained generations across diverse modalities, where each output serves as the subsequent input, to improve membership evidence at low FPR. We demonstrate that memorized training samples exhibit significantly higher coherence and slower degradation during iterative regeneration than non-member generations. Our results show that MADreMIA provides richer signals across diverse model families and modalities; we present comprehensive evaluations for IARs, diffusion, and language models, alongside preliminary results demonstrating its potential for audio models.

</details>


### [234] [Radial Suppression Accelerates Algorithmic Generalization: A Geometric Analysis of Delayed Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.32000)
*Srijan Tiwari,Aditya Chauhan,Manjot Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates why neural networks memorize algorithmic patterns before generalizing, identifying radial inflation of hidden representations under cross-entropy loss as the key cause. A norm penalty that constrains activations to a √d-radius hypersphere reduces memorization delay, accelerating "grokking" up to 6x in modular arithmetic and halving training steps for nanoGPT on 3-digit addition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the memorization-generalization delay (grokking phenomenon) in neural networks and explain why models memorize algorithmic training data long before they actually generalize.

Method: The authors propose a radial-angular decomposition of activation-space dynamics, derive three testable propositions about radial inflation, and validate these with a single-hyperparameter norm penalty that constrains activations to a √d-radius hypersphere.

Result: The norm penalty accelerates grokking up to 6x across MLPs and Transformers on modular arithmetic tasks, and halves the training steps for a 10M-parameter nanoGPT on 3-digit addition.

Conclusion: Radial inflation of hidden representations under cross-entropy optimization is a primary driver of the memorization-generalization delay; constraining it significantly improves learning efficiency.

Abstract: Why do neural networks memorize algorithmic training data long before they generalize? We present a geometric case study demonstrating that, on tasks where generalization requires discovering structured low-dimensional circuits, the memorization-generalization delay is driven by radial inflation of hidden representations under cross-entropy optimization. We formalize a radial-angular decomposition of activation-space dynamics and derive three testable propositions: (i) that penalizing radial inflation induces anisotropic, data-dependent weight regularization; (ii) that it suppresses radial gradient energy below the isotropic random baseline, forcing predominantly angular updates; and (iii) that it biases convergence toward flatter minima. To empirically validate these propositions, we study a single-hyperparameter norm penalty that softly constrains activations to a sqrt(d)-radius hypersphere. On modular arithmetic, this penalty accelerates grokking up to 6x across MLPs and Transformers, and halves training steps for a 10M-parameter nanoGPT on 3-digit addition.

</details>


### [235] [Surrogate Fidelity: When Can Open LLMs Explain Closed Ones?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.32008)
*Philippe Chlenski,Zachariah Carmichael,Ayush Warikoo,Chia-Tse Shao,Yingxiao Ye,Aobo Yang,Vivek Miglani,Nehal Bandi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether measurements on open models can reliably inform claims about closed models for mechanistic interpretability. It finds that prediction-level agreement between models does not imply attribution-level agreement, meaning black-box input ablations are more reliable for causal insight than white-box signals like attention patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability requires full access to model internals, but most widely deployed language models only expose limited APIs (e.g., log-probabilities). This creates a need to evaluate whether surrogate open models can serve as reliable proxies for closed models.

Method: The authors evaluate surrogate fidelity at three levels: prediction, attribution, and representation. They use binary classification tasks with log-odds as API-compatible scalar readouts, and compare leave-one-out attributions across 11 models from 4 families (Llama, Qwen, GPT, Gemini).

Result: Prediction fidelity substantially overstates attribution fidelity — models agreeing on answers often disagree on why. An "access-validity inversion" is documented: white-box signals (attention patterns, perturbation magnitudes) are highly stable but weakly predictive of causal attributions, while black-box input ablations capture attributions by design.

Conclusion: Mechanistic insight does not automatically transfer to closed targets. Prediction-level agreement is insufficient to warrant transferring mechanistic interpretations from open to closed models.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability (MI) requires full access to model internals, yet the APIs for most widely deployed language models at best expose log-probabilities over output tokens. This creates a surrogate problem: when do measurements made on open models allow us to make claims about a closed model? We evaluate surrogate fidelity at the prediction, attribution, and representation levels. For binary classification tasks, log-odds provide an API-compatible scalar readout of the model's representation space, and leave-one-out attributions provide insight into model behavior. Across eleven models spanning four families (Llama, Qwen, GPT, and Gemini), we find that prediction fidelity substantially overstates attribution fidelity: models that agree on what the answer is often disagree on why. We document an access-validity inversion: white-box signals like attention patterns and perturbation magnitudes are highly stable across models but only weakly predictive of causal attributions, which black-box input ablations capture by design. Mechanistic insight does not automatically transfer to closed targets, and prediction-level agreement is insufficient to warrant such transfer. Code and results are available at https://github.com/facebookresearch/surrogate.

</details>


### [236] [CoMet: Context and Multiplicity Decomposition for Multimodal Uncertainty Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.32012)
*Sanghyuk Chun,William Yang,Amaya Dharmasiri,Olga Russakovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoMet proposes an efficient uncertainty estimation method for multimodal LLMs by decomposing uncertainty into context-specific and multiplicity-specific terms, using a lightweight post-hoc module without needing autoregressive generation or repeated sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty estimation (metacognition) is a long-standing challenge in AI, especially for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) where uncertainty can stem from diverse sources and their relationships, compounded by open-ended answer settings.

Method: CoMet decomposes uncertainty into (1) a context-specific term capturing task/prompt ambiguity, and (2) a multiplicity-specific term capturing how many compatible answers exist given the context and input. A lightweight post-hoc uncertainty module is trained to estimate these quantities efficiently.

Result: CoMet consistently improves uncertainty estimation over existing baselines across open-ended multimodal benchmarks, hallucination detection, and multiple-choice VQA, while remaining efficient (no autoregressive generation or repeated sampling needed).

Conclusion: Decomposing uncertainty into context and multiplicity components with a lightweight post-hoc module provides an effective and efficient approach for uncertainty estimation in MLLMs.

Abstract: Uncertainty estimation has been a long-standing challenge in AI models; it amounts to "knowing what you don't know," and metacognition is notoriously difficult even for humans (cf. the Dunning-Kruger effect). Although it is still far from solved even in simpler classification systems, tackling it in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is becoming increasingly important. Within MLLMs, uncertainty can stem from any of the diverse sources as well as from their relationships, and further can stem from the unbounded answers in the open-ended setting. To tackle the issues, we propose CoMet, an MLLM uncertainty estimation method by decomposing uncertainty into a context-specific term and a multiplicity-specific term. The former captures ambiguity induced by the given context (e.g., task or prompt), while the latter captures how many plausible answers determined by the context remain compatible with the given input. We train a lightweight post-hoc uncertainty module to estimate these quantities, which enables efficient uncertainty estimation without autoregressive answer generation or repeated sampling. Experiments on various open-ended multimodal benchmarks, hallucination detection, and multiple-choice visual question answering benchmarks show that CoMet consistently improves uncertainty estimation over existing baselines while remaining efficient in practice. Code is available at https://github.com/princetonvisualai/comet_uncertainty

</details>


### [237] [FedLAB: Traceable Semantic Codebooks for Federated Multimodal Graph Foundation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.32016)
*Zekai Chen,Kairui Yang,Xuaner Chen,Xunkai Li,Xun Wu,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedLAB is a federated multimodal graph foundation learning framework that uses typed hierarchical codebooks to enable traceable semantic knowledge sharing across decentralized clients while preserving privacy, achieving up to 7.53% improvement over baselines on 10 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal graph data (text, images, attributes, topology) is distributed across decentralized clients and cannot be centrally shared due to privacy constraints. Existing federated graph methods exchange parameters/prototypes/embeddings but lack explicit semantic traceability—they don't show how modality evidence, node semantics, and topology context jointly contribute to predictions.

Method: FedLAB organizes multimodal graph knowledge into typed hierarchical codebooks (modality evidence, node semantics, topology context) and refines them via federated semantic barycenter pre-training, keeping raw contents and structures local.

Result: Tested on 10 benchmarks and 6 downstream tasks, FedLAB outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by up to 7.53% while providing a native semantic trace interface.

Conclusion: FedLAB bridges the gap in federated multimodal graph learning by enabling both strong performance and explicit traceability of how multimodal semantics and topology drive predictions, without compromising data privacy.

Abstract: Multimodal graph foundation models aim to learn reusable knowledge from graphs enriched with text, images, attributes, and relational topology, thereby supporting diverse graph-centric and modality-centric tasks. In practice, however, such multimodal graphs are often distributed across decentralized clients, where raw contents and local structures cannot be centrally shared due to privacy constraints. This motivates federated multimodal graph foundation learning, which requires not only transferable representation learning but also intrinsic semantic traceability under strict data isolation. Existing methods usually exchange or store knowledge through parameters, prototypes, embeddings, or compact codebooks, which support optimization and transfer but do not explicitly expose how modality evidence, node semantics, and topology context jointly support predictions. To bridge this gap, we propose FedLAB, a traceable semantic codebook framework that organizes multimodal graph knowledge into typed hierarchical codebooks for modality evidence, node semantics, and topology context. FedLAB further refines these trace units through federated semantic barycenter pre-training while keeping raw multimodal contents and graph structures local. Extensive experiments on 10 benchmarks and 6 downstream tasks show that FedLAB improves over state-of-the-art baselines by up to 7.53\%, while preserving a native semantic trace interface.

</details>


### [238] [TRIAGE: Role-Typed Credit Assignment for Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.32017)
*Yuanda Xu,Zhengze Zhou,Hejian Sang,Xiaomin Li,Jiaxin Zhang,Xinchen Du,Zhipeng Wang,Alborz Geramifard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRIAGE improves GRPO by adding role-typed segment-level process rewards that distinguish decisive progress, useful exploration, no-progress, and regression, correcting blind spots in outcome-only credit assignment for agentic RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard GRPO uses final verifier outcomes as a uniform advantage over all action tokens, which punishes useful exploration in failed rollouts and reinforces redundant/regressive actions in successful ones.

Method: TRIAGE uses a structured judge to classify segments into four semantic roles (decisive progress, useful exploration, no-progress infrastructure, regression), then applies fixed role-conditioned rules to map these labels to bounded segment-level process rewards, providing a role-typed credit assignment correction over outcome-only GRPO.

Result: Across ALFWorld, Search-QA, and WebShop, TRIAGE improves success rates over GRPO for two policy models, outperforms scalar judge-derived process reward and outcome-supervised value baselines, and reduces environment-facing turns by 10.4% and 14.8% on completed rollouts.

Conclusion: Role-typed credit assignment effectively corrects blind spots in outcome-only RL; the dominant gain comes from detecting regression inside successful trajectories, with exploration credit providing consistent secondary improvement.

Abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning requires assigning credit to environment-facing actions such as searches, clicks, edits, navigation commands, and object interactions. Standard GRPO uses the final verifier outcome as a uniform advantage over all action tokens. This outcome signal is useful but structurally incomplete: it punishes useful exploration in failed rollouts and reinforces redundant or regressive actions in successful rollouts. We propose TRIAGE, a role-typed credit assignment framework that adds a semantic role axis to outcome credit. A structured judge classifies each segment as decisive progress, useful exploration, no-progress infrastructure, or regression, and a fixed role-conditioned rule maps these labels to bounded segment-level process rewards. This keeps verifier outcomes as the source of optimization direction while correcting the two main blind spots of outcome-only credit. We further show that role-conditioned credit is the optimal segment-level correction expressible from role labels alone -- a projection of the per-segment advantage residual onto the role variable -- so that the fixed role constants reduce advantage estimation error whenever the judge is reliable, and we connect this to lower-variance policy gradients. Across ALFWorld, Search-QA, and WebShop, TRIAGE improves success rates over GRPO for two policy models and outperforms both a scalar judge-derived process reward and an outcome-supervised shared-backbone value baseline. Ablations show that the gain comes from role typing rather than merely adding dense rewards: reliable detection of regression inside successful trajectories is the dominant contributor, while exploration credit provides a consistent secondary gain; on completed ALFWorld and WebShop rollouts, TRIAGE also reduces environment-facing turns by an additional $10.4\%$ and $14.8\%$ relative to GRPO.

</details>


### [239] [SemRF: A Semantic Reference Frame for Residual-Stream Dynamics in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.32022)
*Jian Gu,Aldeida Aleti,Chunyang Chen,Hongyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Semantic Reference Frames (SemRF), a formalism that separates semantic measurement from residual dynamics in language models. By fixing anchors and using pseudo-inverse tying, SemRF stabilizes semantic coordinates across layers, enabling analysis of residual computation as a depthwise trajectory. The framework defines Voronoi traces, tube constraints, and minimum-action paths to characterize semantic motion, curvature, and compressibility, linking trace complexity to parameter efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard residual-stream analysis suffers from measurement drift because embedding anchors and unembedding readout may disagree on the chosen span, making it unclear whether apparent motion across layers reflects genuine computation or coordinate-system artifacts.

Method: Semantic Reference Frames (SemRF) fix anchor points and measure states against them. Pseudo-inverse tying synchronizes coordinates exactly; restricted bi-invertibility ensures stable semantic-basis coordinates with distortion bounds. The anchors define a semantic Voronoi diagram, and residual computation is framed as a depthwise trajectory. A margin-relaxed tube and minimum-action path within it characterize layerwise steps.

Result: The canonical trace (minimum-action path) is unique when nonempty with positive quadratic weight, obeying a discrete spline equation. Low curvature implies piecewise-linear compressibility and lower trace complexity (fewer semantic knots). Lower-action, lower-complexity traces use fewer semantic degrees of freedom.

Conclusion: SemRF provides a principled way to separate semantic measurement from residual dynamics, with formal guarantees on stability, distortion, and efficiency. The framework offers diagnostics (imbalance, step, curvature) and a conditional link between trace complexity and parameter efficiency under explicit tube constraints.

Abstract: Residual-stream analysis asks how language-model computation evolves across depth, but intermediate decoding requires comparable readout coordinates across layers. If embedding anchors and unembedding readout disagree on the chosen span, apparent motion may reflect measurement drift rather than computation. We introduce \emph{Semantic Reference Frames} (SemRF), an anchor-based formalism separating semantic measurement from residual dynamics. A SemRF fixes anchors and measures states against them. Pseudo-inverse tying gives exact synchronization; under restricted bi-invertibility, SemRF yields stable semantic-basis coordinates, distortion bounds, and near-identity changes. With the frame fixed, residual computation becomes a depthwise semantic trajectory. The anchors induce a semantic Voronoi diagram: distance, or evidence such as logits, assigns each layer to a coarse cell, while coordinates retain within-cell motion and margins.
