Leveraging this shift, we introduce an agentic autoformalization framework powered by general coding LLMs. At the core of our system is an orchestrator that manages a multi-agent pipeline tailored for research-level mathematics. Because cutting-edge research frequently relies on concepts outside the scope of existing libraries like Mathlib, our system dynamically extends necessary type definitions and validates them via a novel Auxiliary Lemma technique before formalizing the primary theorems. We applied our approach to PutnamBench, producing machine-checked Lean proofs for a random sample of 32 problems. Furthermore, we evaluate our system on five papers from the ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing (STOC) spanning combinatorics, communication complexity, mechanism design, and learning theory, successfully formalizing their main theorems and validating the generated formalizations with human experts; for all five we also formalize the proofs alongside the statements, and notably two of them are proved with no axioms beyond Lean's kernel. All of our formalizations are available at https://beyondthelibrary.github.io/formal_arxiv .

</details>


### [266] [Cross-Domain Feature Expansion for Tabular Medical Data via Knowledge Graphs Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31171)
*Mengying Zhou,Yongjie Yin,Haoyan Xin,Guoping Liu,Yang Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MedKGTab is a knowledge-injected framework for cross-domain feature expansion in tabular medical data, using a row-column dual-attention mechanism and SPOKE biomedical knowledge graph to infer uncollected features from available ones, outperforming SOTA medical and tabular models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Acquiring comprehensive cross-domain biomedical profiles is costly and time-consuming, leading to severe data scarcity in medical research.

Method: MedKGTab uses a row-column dual-attention mechanism on raw tabular data, integrates data-driven statistical priors with the SPOKE biomedical knowledge graph, and modulates data-channel representations with injected biomedical knowledge.

Result: MedKGTab achieves high data fidelity and realistic data representation in cross-domain feature expansion, outperforming both SOTA medical large models (e.g., Baichuan M3-plus) and specialized tabular models for medical data generation. It performs well across various scenarios including missing feature inference and cross-cohort generalization.

Conclusion: MedKGTab effectively addresses data scarcity in medical research through knowledge-injected cross-domain feature expansion, demonstrating superior performance over existing models.

Abstract: Acquiring comprehensive cross-domain biomedical profiles is often costly and time-consuming, resulting in severe data scarcity in medical research. To address this challenge, we propose MedKGTab, a knowledge-injected framework specifically engineered for cross-domain feature expansion in tabular medical data. MedKGTab seeks to infer uncollected biomedical features from available ones by exploiting their inherent statistical dependencies and established medical correlations. By employing a row-column dual-attention mechanism, MedKGTab operates directly on raw structured tabular data, inherently capturing exact numerical distributions without the structural loss caused by tokenization. Crucially, MedKGTab integrates data-driven statistical priors with the SPOKE biomedical knowledge graph, achieving an optimal synergy between the data and knowledge channels. Within this synergy, the representations derived from the data channel are modulated by the injected biomedical knowledge, ensuring the final generated data are grounded in empirical medical research. Experimental results demonstrate that MedKGTab achieves high data fidelity and realistic data representation in cross-domain feature expansion. It outperforms both SOTA medical large models (e.g., Baichuan M3-plus) and specialized tabular models designed for medical data generation. Furthermore, MedKGTab consistently delivers superior performance across various data generation scenarios, whether inferring missing features within the same dataset or generalizing across different medical cohorts.

</details>


### [267] [ClawArena-Team: Benchmarking Subagent Orchestration and Dynamic Workflows in Language-Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31174)
*Kaiwen Xiong,Haonian Ji,Shi Qiu,Zeyu Zheng,Cihang Xie,Xinyu Ye,Huaxiu Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ClawArena-Team is a benchmark that isolates and measures the management ability of LLM agents acting as leaders orchestrating subagents, revealing that privilege granting is the main bottleneck, cost and management quality are decoupled, and leaderboard scores cluster narrowly while orchestration behaviors vary widely.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate either a single agent's direct task-solving ability or a fixed multi-agent system's emergent behavior, but none isolate the management skill of a single LLM acting as a leader that delegates and orchestrates subagents. This gap needs filling as LLM agents are increasingly deployed as managers.

Method: The authors introduce ClawArena-Team, a benchmark of 41 multi-turn, multimodal, multi-directory scenarios (258 evaluation rounds, 72 staged updates). The main agent is constrained to text-only perception and limited workspace access, commanding a fixed subagent pool. Scoring is execution-based via SMS (Subagent-Management Score) = task correctness × least-privilege × modality-routing factor.

Result: No model exceeds 50% workspace-permission precision (privilege granting is the key bottleneck). API cost spans over 100× while SMS spans under 4× (cost and quality decoupled; cheapest open models on Pareto frontier). Leaderboard scores cluster within a 9.9-point band but orchestration behaviors diverge by over an order of magnitude.

Conclusion: Management ability in LLM agents can be measured separately from raw capability; privilege granting is the primary bottleneck; cost-efficient open models can match expensive ones in management quality; and aggregate scores mask diverse underlying orchestration behaviors.

Abstract: Production large language-model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed not as lone problem-solvers but as managers: a main model creates specialized subagents, delegates work, and orchestrates their parallel, asynchronous returns through dynamic workflows. Whether one model can actually run such a team is largely unmeasured: existing benchmarks score a policy's own task-solving or a fixed multi-agent system's emergent behavior, but none isolate the management ability of the single LLM acting as leader. We introduce ClawArena-Team, a benchmark of 41 multi-turn, multimodal, multi-directory scenarios spanning 258 evaluation rounds and 72 staged updates that measures this management ability. The main agent is deliberately constrained: it natively perceives only text and directly accesses only part of the workspace. It commands a fixed, locally served subagent pool, so score differences reflect management skill, not raw capability. All scoring is execution-based with no LLM judge: an overall score -- the Subagent-Management Score (SMS) -- multiplies task correctness by a least-privilege and modality-routing factor. Across twelve proprietary, community-hosted, and self-hosted models, experiments show that the management bottleneck is privilege granting rather than perception (no model exceeds 50% workspace-permission precision); that cost and management quality are decoupled (API cost spans over 100 times while the overall score spans under 4 times, with the cheapest open models on the Pareto frontier); and that most leaderboard scores cluster within a 9.9-point band while orchestration behaviors diverge by more than an order of magnitude. Code and data will be released.

</details>


### [268] [HealthAgentBench: A Unified Benchmark Suite of Realistic Agentic Healthcare Environments for Challenging Frontier AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31179)
*Qianchu Liu,Sheng Zhang,Guanghui Qin,Jeya Maria Jose Valanarasu,Maximilian Rokuss,Mingyu Lu,Timothy Ossowski,Juan Manuel Zambrano Chaves,Cliff Wong,Peniel Argaw,Yashna Hasija,Mu Wei,Wen-wai Yim,Qin Liu,Zilin Jing,Jason Entenmann,Naoto Usuyama,Tristan Naumann,Hoifung Poon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HealthAgentBench is a benchmark of 54 agentic healthcare tasks across 7 categories. Frontier agents achieve at most ~42% success rate (Codex GPT-5.5), revealing significant room for improvement, especially in medical imaging and compositional reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI agents advance in complex reasoning, there is a need for rigorous, holistic evaluation benchmarks that measure progress toward real-world healthcare applications, particularly for end-to-end clinical workflows.

Method: The authors introduce 54 agentic healthcare tasks across 7 categories, each with a unique environment. Tasks replicate end-to-end clinical workflows where agents must explore raw data, operate in complex environments, and execute multi-step solutions. Performance is measured by a final task success rate metric.

Result: The best agent (Codex GPT-5.5) achieves ~42% success rate, indicating the benchmark's difficulty. Medical imaging is especially challenging for Claude Code models; tasks combining large search spaces with compositional reasoning remain difficult for all agents.

Conclusion: HealthAgentBench provides a challenging and realistic benchmark for evaluating AI agents in healthcare, with substantial room for future progress. The benchmark is publicly released.

Abstract: As AI agents become increasingly capable of complex, long-horizon reasoning, rigorous and holistic evaluation is essential for measuring progress toward real-world healthcare applications. We introduce HealthAgentBench, a suite of 54 agentic healthcare tasks across 7 categories each with its unique environment. The benchmark suite spans diverse workflows throughout the patient journey and a broad range of modalities. Each task is designed to replicate an end-to-end clinical workflow: given minimal instructions, an agent must explore raw healthcare data, operate within a complex environment, and execute multi-step solutions that go beyond naive prompting. A final task success rate is reported to provide a single, interpretable metric for HealthAgentBench overall performance for each agent. Evaluating frontier agents on HealthAgentBench, we find that overall task success rate remains low, underscoring the difficulty of the suite. The strongest and the most cost effective agent, Codex GPT-5.5, achieves only approximately 42% success rate. Beyond aggregate performance, HealthAgentBench reveals nuanced strengths and weaknesses across task categories. Frontier agents show promise in automatically developing research modeling pipelines over EHR data, but medical imaging remains especially challenging, particularly for Claude Code models, while Codex GPT-5.5 shows emerging capability. Tasks that combine large search spaces with compositional reasoning requirements remain difficult for all current agents. Together, these results suggest that HealthAgentBench provides a challenging and realistic benchmark with substantial room for future progress. We release our benchmark at https://github.com/microsoft/HealthAgentBench.

</details>


### [269] [AI-Assisted Discovery of Convex Relaxations via Dual Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31182)
*Sungyoon Kim,Mert Pilanci*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents an automated system that discovers convex relaxations to derive certified lower bounds for sharp-constant inequalities, improving two known constants via an "autoresearch" paradigm with coding and theory agents verified by interval arithmetic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agent work focuses on finding upper bounds for sharp-constant inequalities by searching for extremal constructions. The complementary problem of deriving lower bounds (which hold for every admissible function) remains underexplored and requires a different approach based on convex relaxations.

Method: The authors instantiate an "autoresearch" paradigm with: (1) a coding agent that proposes valid tightening constraints for convex relaxations, (2) a theory agent that verifies constraints and searches for counterexamples, and (3) certification of every reported bound via explicit dual-feasible points checked with rigorous interval arithmetic.

Result: On two constants from prior work—the first autocorrelation inequality ($C_{6.2}$) and the Erdős minimum-overlap constant ($C_{6.5}$)—the certified lower bounds are improved from 1.28 to 1.2937 and from 0.379005 to 0.37912, respectively.

Conclusion: The autoresearch approach successfully discovers tighter convex relaxations to improve lower bounds on optimization constants, complementing existing upper-bound search methods and providing formally certified results.

Abstract: Recent work shows that LLM agents can improve sharp-constant inequalities by searching for extremal constructions, which yield upper bounds. We address the complementary side: a lower bound holds for every admissible function and follows from a convex relaxation of the nonconvex problem, with tighter relaxations giving stronger bounds. We instantiate the autoresearch paradigm to discover such relaxations: a coding agent proposes valid tightening constraints, a theory agent verifies each one and searches for counterexamples, and every reported bound is certified by an explicit dual-feasible point checked in rigorous interval arithmetic. On two optimization constants studied by \citet{tao2025alphaevolve} - the first autocorrelation inequality ($C_{6.2}$) and the Erdős minimum-overlap constant ($C_{6.5}$) - we improve the certified lower bounds from $1.28$ to $1.2937$ and from $0.379005$ to $0.37912$, respectively.

</details>


### [270] [Agentic RAG-VLM: Affordance-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Self-Reflective Planning for Robotic Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31200)
*Tao Chen,Lizheng Liu,Jiaxu Wang,Ziyue Jiang,Ruiqi Tian,JiGuang Huo,Zhongxue Gan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic RAG-VLM integrates affordance-aware retrieval, scene graph reasoning, and self-reflective recovery to achieve 78.3% grasping success in cluttered environments, outperforming VLM-only baselines by 53.3 percentage points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM-based grasping methods rely on visual similarity for object matching, neglecting physical affordances (e.g., handle graspability, material fragility) and operate open-loop without spatial reasoning or failure recovery, limiting performance in cluttered, physically diverse environments.

Method: Three components: (1) Hierarchical Affordance-Aware RAG encoding 4D affordance descriptors (type, material, fragility, graspable region); (2) Scene Graph Constraint Reasoner for spatial relationship reasoning; (3) Agentic Self-Reflective Pipeline with 14-type failure taxonomy and three-level adaptive retry for closed-loop refinement.

Result: 78.3% overall success on 12-task benchmark (360 trials per configuration), achieving 53.3 percentage-point absolute gain over VLM-only baselines.

Conclusion: Affordance-aware retrieval, scene graph reasoning, and agentic recovery are jointly essential for robust manipulation in cluttered environments.

Abstract: Generalizable robotic grasping in cluttered environments is essential for deploying manipulators in unstructured human spaces, yet existing VLM-based methods rely on visual similarity for object matching, neglecting physical affordances such as handle graspability and material fragility, and operate open-loop without spatial reasoning or failure recovery, limiting their effectiveness when objects are densely packed or physically diverse. We present Agentic RAG-VLM, a unified framework that bridges VLM-based semantic understanding and physically grounded grasp execution by integrating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with vision-language models (VLMs) and agentic self-reflective planning. Agentic RAG-VLM introduces three tightly coupled components: (1) a Hierarchical Affordance-Aware RAG (HAA-RAG) that encodes four-dimensional affordance descriptors, including type, material, fragility, and graspable region, and retrieves strategies by functional affordance compatibility rather than visual appearance; (2) a Scene Graph Constraint Reasoner that constructs spatial relationship graphs from VLM perception and translates proximity, occlusion, and support constraints into concrete grasp parameter adjustments; and (3) an Agentic Self-Reflective Pipeline with a 14-type failure taxonomy and three-level adaptive retry for closed-loop grasp refinement. Evaluated on a 12-task benchmark spanning single-grasp, interactive, and long-horizon scenarios with 360 trials per configuration, Agentic RAG-VLM achieves 78.3 percent overall success, a 53.3 percentage-point absolute gain over VLM-only baselines, demonstrating that affordance-aware retrieval, scene graph reasoning, and agentic recovery are jointly essential for robust manipulation.

</details>


### [271] [Towards Inclusive Mobility Modeling: Characterizing and Evaluating Elderly Trajectory Patterns in Urban Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31207)
*Zhengxuan Wang,Haohan He,Mengying Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This study shows that elderly riders have distinct mobility patterns (shorter trips, lower entropy, off-peak timing) and that trajectory models trained on majority-young populations systematically misrepresent elderly behavior, with higher-capability models like LLMs not necessarily improving subgroup fidelity under limited demographic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address how the underrepresentation of elderly populations in mobility datasets introduces systematic bias into mobility modeling and downstream urban planning decisions.

Method: Analyzed Citi Bike data (2016-2020 Jersey City); compared Markov chain and Qwen3-4B (QLoRA fine-tuned) models trained on full population, young-only, and elderly-only data; evaluated synthetic trajectory fidelity across demographic groups.

Result: Elderly riders show distinct mobility signatures (958m vs 1,189m step length; 1.82 vs 4.15 entropy). Full-population Markov model overestimates elderly step length by 4.5% and dwell time by 8.9%. Elderly-specific models reduce errors. LLMs do not guarantee better subgroup fidelity with limited demographic data.

Conclusion: Demographic representation is critical for fair mobility modeling; relying on majority-dominated data yields biased outcomes, and model capacity alone cannot compensate for missing subgroup data.

Abstract: The rapid advance of smart cities increasingly depends on trajectory data mining, yet underrepresented demographic groups, particularly the elderly, are often sparsely represented in public mobility datasets. This underrepresentation can introduce systematic bias into mobility modeling and downstream urban planning. Using the 2016-2020 Jersey City subset of the Citi Bike System Data, this study quantitatively examines how the absence of underrepresented subgroups' mobility signatures affects mobility modeling, using synthetic trajectory generation as a case study. The analysis reveals that elderly riders exhibit a structurally distinct mobility signature, including localized activity spaces (958 m vs. 1,189 m for young riders), lower mobility entropy (1.82 vs. 4.15), and asymmetric off-peak temporal patterns. To demonstrate that relying on majority-dominated training data yields biased synthetic outcomes, we further evaluate both a first-order Markov chain and a Qwen3-4B model fine-tuned with QLoRA across three demographic training settings: the full population, young riders only, and elderly riders only. Results show that models trained on majority-dominated populations systematically misrepresent elderly mobility behavior, particularly for spatial mobility metrics. The Markov model trained on the full population overestimates elderly step length by 4.5% and dwell time by 8.9%, whereas the elderly-specific model achieves substantially lower errors across most metrics. Comparisons between the Markov and LLM-based frameworks further show that higher-capability models do not necessarily improve subgroup-level fidelity under limited demographic data. These findings underscore the importance of demographic representation in mobility modeling and its downstream applications for underrepresented populations.

</details>


### [272] [Long-term Traffic Simulation via Structured Autoregressive Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31209)
*Lingyu Xiao,Zexin Feng,Xintao Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RosettaSim leverages LLM inductive biases and statistical priors to achieve state-of-the-art traffic simulation, with RTE improving long-horizon evaluation alignment (r=0.83 vs 0.74).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon traffic simulation struggles with modeling sustained multi-agent interactions and dynamic token cardinality as agents enter/exit scenes.

Method: RosettaSim projects scene topology, agent states, and spawning intents into a structured autoregressive stream with variable length, leveraging small-scale frozen LLMs. RTE uses retrieval-based evaluation with semantically similar real-world references.

Result: State-of-the-art on WOSAC for both short- and long-term simulation. RTE achieves r=0.83 correlation with standard metrics vs. existing r=0.74.

Conclusion: Synergy between LLM architectural biases and traffic modeling priors enables effective long-horizon simulation; RTE better aligns with simulation fidelity.

Abstract: Interactive traffic simulation is a vital world model for autonomous driving. A central challenge in long-horizon simulation is modeling sustained multi-agent interactions, which is further exacerbated by dynamic token cardinality as agents continuously enter and exit the scene. In this work, we propose that the solution lies in the synergy between the architectural inductive biases and statistical priors of large-scale sequence models, e.g., Large Language Models (LLMs). Our probing experiments reveal that the transferability of attention mechanisms and the distributional consistency between motion tokens and natural language enable small-scale, heavily frozen LLMs to rapidly adapt to traffic modeling. Building on this insight, we introduce RosettaSim, a unified framework that projects scene topology, agent states, and spawning intents into a structured autoregressive stream with variable length, achieving both strong short-term accuracy and stable long-horizon simulation fidelity. Furthermore, evaluating extended rollouts presents yet another hurdle, as one-to-one agent correspondence inevitably fades over time. To address this, we introduce Retrieval-based Traffic Evaluation (RTE), which retrieves semantically similar real-world scenarios as context-aware reference anchors. Experiments on the Waymo Open Sim Agent Challenge (WOSAC) demonstrate that RosettaSim achieves state-of-the-art performance in both short- and long-term simulation. Furthermore, RTE exhibits a stronger correlation with standard metrics ($r=0.83$) than existing approaches ($r=0.74$), indicating improved alignment with long-horizon simulation fidelity.

</details>


### [273] [Thinking Before Retrieving: Robust Zero-Shot Composed Image Retrieval via Strategic Planning and Self-Criticism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31222)
*Gunho Jung,Jeong-Woo Park,Seon Bin Kim,Seong-Whan Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PEC-CIR is a training-free framework for composed image retrieval that replaces single-pass query generation with a multi-stage Planner-Executor-Critic pipeline to reduce semantic errors and improve retrieval precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-pass generation strategies for composed image retrieval suffer from semantic distortions, omissions, and interference between reference attribute preservation and textual requirement integration, degrading retrieval precision.

Method: PEC-CIR employs a Planner-Executor-Critic architecture: Planner extracts explicit constraints, Executor generates multiple candidate target descriptions, and Critic evaluates candidates based on constraint compliance in a staged inference process.

Result: The multi-stage pipeline reduces propagation of generative errors by evaluating candidate queries before retrieval, improving retrieval stability compared to single-pass approaches.

Conclusion: Reframing query construction as staged inference rather than single-pass output enhances composed image retrieval in a training-free zero-shot setting.

Abstract: Composed image retrieval requires identifying a target image from a gallery by integrating a reference image with a textual modification instruction. In a training-free zero-shot setting, this task relies on constructing a retrieval-oriented textual query within a frozen vision--language embedding space at inference time. Existing approaches predominantly rely on a single-pass generation strategy that fuses the reference context and modification text into a unified description. This strategy makes it difficult to detect or correct semantic distortions and omissions during generation. Consequently, the preservation of reference attributes and the integration of textual requirements interfere with each other, which degrades retrieval precision. To address these challenges, we introduce PEC-CIR, a training-free framework that structures query construction as a multi-stage reasoning pipeline. The framework operates through a Planner--Executor--Critic architecture where the Planner extracts explicit constraints, the Executor generates multiple candidate target descriptions, and the Critic evaluates these candidates based on constraint compliance. By reframing query construction as a staged inference process instead of a single-pass output, PEC-CIR reduces the propagation of generative errors by explicitly evaluating candidate queries before retrieval, thereby improving retrieval stability.

</details>


### [274] [Agentic-Ideation: Sample Efficient Agentic Trajectories Synthesis for Scientific Ideation Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31229)
*Keyu Zhao,Lingyan Kong,Fengli Xu,Yong Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic-Ideation proposes an automated trajectory synthesis pipeline and specialized agentic LLM for scientific ideation, using Oracle-Guided Data Synthesis and masking strategies to achieve 11.91% quality improvement over SOTA with 10x better data efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automated ideation systems rely on rigid pre-defined agentic workflows, limiting flexibility in navigating scientific literature and research reasoning. Previous agentic data synthesis methods are prohibitively expensive for scientific ideation.

Method: 1) Defines a tool space with 3 external tools + 3 cognitive tools. 2) Oracle-Guided Data Synthesis uses reference ideas to steer multi-agent systems toward efficient trajectory generation. 3) Trains agent on synthesized trajectories with masking on tool execution results to focus on decision-making logic.

Result: Outperforms SOTA workflow-based baseline by 11.91% in overall quality; improves sample efficiency of high-quality data synthesis by over 10x.

Conclusion: Agentic LLM training with oracle-guided trajectory synthesis is a promising and efficient direction for automated scientific ideation, overcoming the flexibility and cost limitations of prior workflow-based approaches.

Abstract: Ideation plays a pivotal role in scientific discovery. Recent LLM, especially AI Scientist systems, show promising potential for automated ideation. However, existing approaches predominantly rely on pre-defined agentic workflows. This constraint severely limits the flexibility required to navigate the vast search space of scientific literature and the complex action space of research reasoning. Recently, training Agentic LLMs has emerged as a promising direction, offering flexible reasoning frameworks and the capability for autonomous tool utilization. However, there remains a non-trivial challenge: applying previous agentic data synthesis methods to scientific ideation suffers from prohibitively high data synthesis cost. To bridge this gap, we propose Agentic-Ideation, a novel framework comprising an automated trajectory synthesis pipeline and a specialized agentic LLM trained for scientific ideation. Specifically, we first define a comprehensive tool space incorporating three external tools and three cognitive tools. Then we introduce an Oracle-Guided Data Synthesis strategy. By leveraging a reference idea as oracle guidance, this approach steers the multi-agent system to efficiently reconstruct the logical reasoning and tool invocation paths, transforming aimless trial-and-error into directed trajectory generation. Finally, we train the agent on these synthesized trajectories, employing a masking strategy on tool execution results. This ensures the model focuses on decision-making logic without interference from external feedback. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms the SOTA workflow-based baseline by \textbf{11.91\%} in overall quality. Furthermore, our approach improves the sample efficiency of high-quality data synthesis by \textbf{over 10$\times$}.

</details>


### [275] [Delta-JEPA: Learning Action-Sensitive World Models via Latent Difference Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31232)
*Zhenghao Zhang,Yuanxiang Wang,Zhenyu Guan,Yujia Yang,Bingkang Shi,Tianyu Zong,Hongzhu Yi,Guoqing Chao,Xingchen Chen,Tiankun Yang,Chenxi Bao,Tao Yu,Jingjing Zhou,Jungang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Delta-JEPA is a reconstruction-free world model that uses a Latent Difference Action Decoder to prevent representation collapse by reconstructing actions from latent displacement, improving planning in visual control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Joint-embedding objectives without reconstruction can collapse to action-insensitive representations, making latent dynamics useless for planning.

Method: Delta-JEPA augments latent forward prediction with a Latent Difference Action Decoder (LDAD) that reconstructs actions from latent displacement between consecutive observations, regularizing transition geometry.

Result: Delta-JEPA improves planning over JEPA-based and representation-learning baselines across four visual continuous-control tasks; displacement-based decoding outperforms endpoint concatenation.

Conclusion: Supervising latent differences is a simple and effective mechanism for learning collapse-resistant and action-sensitive world models.

Abstract: Learning visual world models for planning requires compact latent dynamics that remain sensitive to actions, yet reconstruction-free joint-embedding objectives can collapse to action-insensitive representations. We propose Delta-JEPA, an end-to-end reconstruction-free world model that augments latent forward prediction with a Latent Difference Action Decoder (LDAD). Unlike inverse decoders that infer actions from concatenated endpoint embeddings, LDAD reconstructs the executed action from the latent displacement between consecutive observations. This displacement-level supervision directly regularizes transition geometry: adjacent embeddings cannot collapse without losing action information, and different actions are encouraged to induce distinguishable latent changes for rollout-based planning. Delta-JEPA uses only latent prediction and action reconstruction, avoiding pixel reconstruction and distribution-matching regularizers. Across four visual continuous-control tasks, Delta-JEPA improves planning over JEPA-based and representation-learning world model baselines. Ablations show that displacement-based action decoding is consistently more effective than endpoint concatenation, and action-sensitivity analyses show clearer action-conditioned latent responses. These results indicate that supervising latent differences is a simple and effective mechanism for collapse-resistant and action-sensitive world model learning.

</details>


### [276] [Embodied CAD: Solver-Grounded LLM Agents for Parametric B-Rep Assembly Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31252)
*Fumin Liu,Haoyu Zhou,Fei Hao,Lin Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Embodied CAD presents solver-grounded LLM agents that iteratively build parametric B-Rep assembly models using a stratified skill library, CAD backend execution, and solver feedback, achieving strong executable rates on mechanical assembly tasks while exposing gaps in long-horizon policy prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can generate syntactically valid CAD scripts, but real industrial modeling requires exact geometric kernel acceptance and parametric editability, which naive script generation fails to guarantee.

Method: The agent iteratively selects actions from a stratified L0-L4 CAD skill library, resolves them into typed geometric operations, executes them in a CAD backend, and uses solver feedback with action grammar constraints, deterministic parameter resolution, and solver-derived rewards for supervised warm-up and GRPO-style refinement.

Result: Solver-grounded planning executes all strong-planner workflows; learned controllers achieve high executable rates but reveal a remaining gap between valid tool calls and exact long-horizon policy prediction.

Conclusion: Grounded solver feedback significantly improves reliability in parametric CAD assembly modeling, but long-horizon exact policy prediction remains an open challenge.

Abstract: Large language models can write plausible CAD scripts, but reliable industrial CAD modeling requires more than syntactically valid code: every feature, placement, and assembly relation must be accepted by an exact geometric kernel while remaining editable as parametric boundary representation geometry. We present Embodied CAD, solver-grounded LLM agents for parametric B-Rep assembly modeling. Instead of generating a complete script in one pass, the agent iteratively selects actions from a stratified L0-L4 CAD skill library, resolves them into typed geometric operations, executes them in a CAD backend, and uses solver feedback to plan, repair, and learn. The framework combines action grammar constraints, deterministic parameter resolution, and solver-derived rewards for supervised warm-up and GRPO-style refinement. We evaluate Embodied CAD on multi-step mechanical, industrial equipment, and mold-oriented assembly tasks using solver-aligned metrics: executable rate, skill accuracy, operation-family accuracy, exact policy accuracy, and task completion success. The results show that solver-grounded planning executes all strong-planner workflows in the current benchmark, while learned controllers reach high executable rates and expose the remaining gap between valid tool calls and exact long-horizon policy prediction.

</details>


### [277] [Spatial Reasoning via Modality Switching Between Language and Symbolic Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31285)
*Shreya Rajpal,Tanawan Premsri,Parisa Kordjamshidi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a switching metric that helps LLMs decide when to shift from natural language reasoning to structured grid-based representations for multi-hop spatial-textual stories, improving accuracy by up to 42%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human reasoning often externalizes complex problems into diagrams or grids; the paper investigates whether grounding textual-spatial stories into geometry-aware modalities improves LLM reasoning over pure language inference.

Method: A switching metric based on trustworthiness and complexity signals determines when to transition from natural language reasoning to structured grid-based representations for spatial reasoning tasks.

Result: Switching from natural language reasoning to grid-based representation improves LLM performance by up to 42% across experimental settings.

Conclusion: Modality choice significantly impacts reasoning outcomes, and principled modality selection—grounding spatial stories into structure when appropriate—can substantially boost LLM performance.

Abstract: Human reasoning is inherently multimodal: when problems become difficult, we rarely think in words alone. We often externalize our reasoning by sketching diagrams or drawing grids to understand the underlying conceptual structure and avoid mistakes. Building on this premise, our research investigates: (a) whether grounding multi-hop textual-spatial stories into geometry-aware modalities, such as layouts or grids, improves reasoning compared to natural language-based inference; and (b) whether a model can decide when to rely on natural language reasoning and when to switch to a structured modality. We address these questions by introducing a switching metric based on trustworthiness and complexity signals, which estimates when grounding a spatial story into structure is likely to improve performance. This takes a first step toward principled modality selection in Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning. Across our settings, switching from natural language-based reasoning to a grid-based representation improves LLM performance by up to 42\%, highlighting the importance of modality choice in shaping reasoning outcomes.

</details>


### [278] [Benchmarking Large Language Models on Floating-Point Error Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31308)
*Lisa Taldir,Muhammad Ahmad Saeed,David Defour,Pablo de Oliveira Castro,Eric Petit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates LLMs' ability to statically detect floating-point errors in code, introducing InterFLOPBench (90 C kernels, 1,130 samples across 6 error types), and finds top LLMs achieve >0.88 F1-score overall, with performance varying significantly by error category.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Floating-point errors are notoriously difficult to detect statically in software, and traditional static analysis tools struggle with this task. The authors aim to explore whether LLMs, which have shown strong code understanding capabilities, can serve as effective detectors for these subtle numerical bugs.

Method: The authors create InterFLOPBench, a benchmark of 90 C kernels with 1,130 test samples covering six floating-point error categories (cancellation, comparison, division by zero, overflow, underflow, NaN). They evaluate 14 LLMs on this benchmark, treating error detection as a multi-label classification problem and using F1-score as the evaluation metric.

Result: Top-performing models (Qwen 3 32b, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Phi 4 Reasoning, DeepSeek R1T2, gpt-oss 20b/120b) achieve >0.88 overall F1-score. However, performance varies significantly: division by zero is easiest (avg F1: 0.8479), while underflow (0.6059) and cancellation (0.6164) are much harder.

Conclusion: LLMs show promising capability in detecting floating-point errors statically, especially for obvious errors like division by zero, but struggle with more subtle numerical phenomena like underflow and cancellation, indicating room for improvement in detecting nuanced floating-point issues.

Abstract: This paper investigates the capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to detect and classify floating-point errors statically in software code. We introduce InterFLOPBench, a benchmark of 90 C kernels with 1 130 test samples designed to evaluate LLMs across six categories of floating-point error: cancellation, comparison, division by zero, overflow, underflow and NaN, compared across 14 LLMs. The evaluation framework treats floating-point error detection as a multi-label classification problem and employs the F1-score metric to measure performance. Results demonstrate that latest models (Qwen 3 32b, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Phi 4 Reasoning, DeepSeek R1T2, and gpt-oss 20b and 120b) achieve a performance greater than 0.88 overall F1-score. Performance varies between error categories, between explicit operations such as division by zero (Average F1-score: 0.8479) and more subtle numerical phenomena such as underflow (Average F1-score: 0.6059) and cancellation (Average F1-score: 0.6164).

</details>


### [279] [HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic: Multi-Hop Question Answering Corpus for Historical Research, Parliamentary Debates from the French Third Republic (1870-1940)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31325)
*Aurélien Pellet,Julien Perez,Marie Puren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic is a new French-language dataset of 1782 multi-hop historical questions built from parliamentary debates and newspapers of the French Third Republic, designed to test retrieval-augmented and LLM systems on complex historical reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between existing NLP benchmarks and the authentic needs of historical scholarship by providing a dataset that captures complex reasoning patterns such as cross-source synthesis, temporal reasoning, and sparse evidence integration.

Method: The corpus was constructed in collaboration with a historian, using carefully selected and aligned sources from parliamentary debates and newspapers, with question validation and metadata integration; the methodology is adaptable to other languages and national corpora.

Result: A dataset of 1782 multi-hop historical questions emphasizing multi-hop connections across heterogeneous historical documents.

Conclusion: The corpus supports realistic evaluation scenarios for multi-hop QA, bridging NLP benchmarks and historical scholarship needs, and the methodology is transferable to other languages and corpora.

Abstract: We present HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic: a French-language dataset of multi-hop historical questions derived from parliamentary debates and newspapers of the French Third Republic. Designed in collaboration with a historian, the corpus captures complex reasoning patterns typical of historical inquiry, including cross-source synthesis, temporal reasoning, and the integration of sparse evidence. The dataset is made of 1782 questions and emphasizes multi-hop connections across heterogeneous historical documents, providing a resource for evaluating retrieval-augmented and large language model systems in domain-specific contexts. We describe the methodology for constructing the corpus, including the selection and alignment of sources, question validation, and metadata integration. While the dataset focuses on French historical documents, our methodology can be readily adapted to other languages and national corpora. Finally, we demonstrate how the corpus can support realistic evaluation scenarios for multi-hop question answering, bridging the gap between NLP benchmarks and the needs of historical scholarship.

</details>


### [280] [CryoACE: An Atom-centric Framework for Accurate and Automated Model Building in Cryo-EM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31332)
*Minzhang Li,Mingrui Li,Weichen Qin,Qihe Chen,Sixian Shen,Yuan Pei,Jiakai Zhang,Jingyi Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CryoACE is an end-to-end framework for protein automodeling from cryo-EM density maps that reconstructs precise atomic graphs for both homogeneous and heterogeneous structures, using atom-centric reconstruction with iterative refinement and training-free guidance from local resolution priors, outperforming baselines on static benchmarks and revealing atomic-level dynamics on real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current cryo-EM protein modeling solvers struggle with enforcing physicochemical validity, managing conformational heterogeneity, and often rely on static predictions or computationally expensive heuristic searches, motivating the need for a more efficient and flexible framework.

Method: CryoACE features an atom-centric reconstruction paradigm that samples density features directly at atomic coordinates and iteratively recycles them to refine structures (replacing expensive voxel convolutions), plus a training-free guidance mechanism using predicted local resolution priors to resolve dynamic ambiguity.

Result: CryoACE significantly outperforms existing baselines on static benchmarks and, for the first time, unveils atomic-level dynamic conformations on complex real-world datasets (e.g., EMPIAR-10345) without pre-built static structures.

Conclusion: CryoACE provides an efficient, end-to-end solution for both homogeneous and heterogeneous cryo-EM modeling, achieving superior performance on static tasks and enabling novel atomic-level dynamic conformation analysis on real-world data.

Abstract: Protein automodeling from cryo-EM density maps faces unique challenges in enforcing physicochemical validity and managing conformational heterogeneity. Current solvers are often limited to static predictions or require computationally intensive heuristic searches. We present CryoACE, an end-to-end framework that reconstructs precise atomic graphs for both homogeneous and heterogeneous structures. Our method features two key innovations: an atom-centric reconstruction paradigm, where density features are sampled directly at atomic coordinates and iteratively recycled to refine structures, replacing expensive voxel convolutions for efficient multimodal fusion; and a training-free guidance mechanism that leverages predicted local resolution priors to resolve dynamic ambiguity. Validated on a newly constructed high-quality dataset, CryoACE significantly outperforms existing baselines on static benchmarks and, for the first time, unveils atomic-level dynamic conformations on complex real-world datasets like EMPIAR-10345 without relying on pre-built static structures.

</details>


### [281] [Optimization Algorithms for Joint OFDM Waveform Design and RIS Configuration in 6G Networks: From Convex Relaxation to Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31334)
*Ahmet Kaplan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A survey of 78 joint OFDM-RIS optimization works (2021-2026) classified into four paradigms, revealing ML methods achieve 95-99% of model-based spectral efficiency at 10^2-10^4x faster inference (GPU N-invariant), six open challenges, and a call for standardized benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Joint OFDM-RIS optimization for 6G involves MINLP problems across multiple objectives (sum-rate, energy efficiency, fairness, PAPR), yet no standardized benchmark exists, making cross-paper comparisons infeasible despite 78 published works.

Method: Survey classifies 78 works (2021-2026) into four paradigms: (I) model-based convex relaxation, (II) heuristic/metaheuristic search, (III) deep RL and unsupervised learning, (IV) emerging methods (FM, diffusion AI, quantum). Includes a companion tutorial benchmark at N=16/64/128 and literature synthesis of self-reported metrics.

Result: ML methods (Paradigm III) report 95-99% of model-based spectral efficiency at 10^2-10^4x faster inference. GPU-based neural inference (DDQN, PPO, GNN, unsupervised DL) shows N-invariant runtime; iterative solvers scale polynomially. Six open challenges identified.

Conclusion: The study serves as a roadmap for 6G OFDM-RIS optimization, specifying requirements for a standardized benchmark and highlighting critical gaps: cross-paradigm benchmarking, hardware constraints, doubly-dispersive channels, PAPR trade-offs, LLM safety, and diminishing returns of heuristics.

Abstract: Joint OFDM-RIS optimization for 6G is a mixed-integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) problem covering sum-rate maximization, energy efficiency, max-min fairness, and peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR)-constrained objectives. Seventy-eight joint OFDM-RIS optimization works published between 2021 and 2026 are surveyed. No standardized benchmark exists, and cross-paper comparisons remain infeasible. This survey classifies these works into four paradigms: (I) model-based convex relaxation, (II) heuristic and metaheuristic search, (III) deep reinforcement and unsupervised learning, and (IV) emerging methods including foundation models (FM), diffusion-based generative AI, and quantum optimization. A literature synthesis of self-reported benchmarks shows that ML-based methods (Paradigm~III) report 95-99\% of model-based spectral efficiency at 10^2-10^4 x faster per-inference runtime (method-pair dependent; literature values are self-reported and exclude ML pre-training cost). A companion tutorial benchmark at N=16, N=64, and N=128 reveals a critical scaling property: GPU-based neural network inference (DDQN, PPO, graph neural network (GNN), unsupervised DL) is N-invariant, with identical runtime at N=16 and N=128, while iterative solvers (AO+SCA, PSO) scale polynomially. Energy efficiency (P2) and PAPR-constrained (P4) benchmarks are deferred to future work with standardized power models and waveform generators. Six open challenges emerge from the synthesis: the cross-paradigm benchmark deficit, real-world hardware-constrained deployment, joint waveform-RIS optimization for doubly-dispersive channels, multi-objective PAPR trade-offs, LLM safety in live network control, and diminishing returns of standalone heuristics. We specify requirements for a standardized benchmark. This study serves as a roadmap for researchers and practitioners working on joint OFDM-RIS optimization in 6G networks.

</details>


### [282] [Smart charging of large fleets of Electric Vehicles: Independent Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning approaches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31347)
*Xavier Rate,Eloann Le Guern,Raphaël Féraud,Fatma Salem,Melissa Chiknoun,Eymeric Giabicani,Mehdi Feki,Patrick Maillé,Guy Camilleri,Anne Blavette,Hamid Benhamed*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper compares two multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches (contextual combinatorial bandits and policy gradient algorithms) for decentralized EV charging coordination to minimize costs and prevent grid overloads, evaluated under realistic conditions with dynamic pricing from real solar data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electric vehicle adoption creates power grid challenges (peak demand, voltage issues, renewable integration) requiring efficient, decentralized EV charging coordination that minimizes user costs while avoiding network overloads.

Method: The authors compare two multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches: (1) contextual combinatorial bandits and (2) policy gradient algorithms, using a realistic simulation where autonomous agents make decisions based on local information (price signals, state-of-charge, temporal constraints), evaluated across varying congestion levels and mixed-strategy configurations under dynamic pricing from real photovoltaic data.

Result: The paper evaluates performance across different congestion levels and heterogeneous agent groups with mixed strategies, using dynamic electricity pricing derived from real photovoltaic production data.

Conclusion: Contextual combinatorial bandits and policy gradient algorithms offer viable but differently performing approaches for implicit coordination in decentralized EV charging, with implications for cost-efficient grid management under renewable energy integration.

Abstract: The electrification of transportation through electric vehicles introduces new challenges for power grid management, such as increased peak demand, voltage fluctuations, line overloads, and the integration of variable renewable energy sources. To enable efficient integration of EVs while minimizing costs for users and avoiding network overloads, implicit coordination between EVs is required. This work compares two independent multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches for optimizing such decentralized EV charging: contextual combinatorial bandits and policy gradient algorithms. Using a realistic simulation environment with autonomous agents making decisions based on local environmental information (including price signals, state-of-charge, and temporal constraints), we evaluate their performance across varying congestion levels, and mixed-strategy configurations with heterogeneous agent groups under dynamic electricity pricing derived from real photovoltaic production data.

</details>


### [283] [ReGRPO: Reflection-Augmented Policy Optimization for Tool-Using Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31392)
*Binjie Zhang,Mike Zheng Shou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReGRPO enhances tool-augmented VLMs by using structured reflection data and joint optimization of reflection tokens and corrective actions within local trajectories, outperforming baselines on GTA and GAIA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tool-augmented VLMs are fragile due to SFT trained mostly on successful trajectories with no recovery signals, and RL rewards that provide only sparse trajectory-level feedback without step-level guidance on failures.

Method: (1) Structured reflective data engine with near-miss actions to collect failure observations and build Reflection-of-Thought triplets (ErrorType, Evidence, FixPlan) for warm-start SFT; (2) Joint optimization of reflection tokens and corrective actions via group-relative advantages within local trajectories; (3) Inclusion of a reflection-cost term to reduce unnecessary reflection.

Result: ReGRPO consistently outperforms strong open-source baselines under the same backbone and tool suite, achieving the best results among compared open-source controllers on GTA and GAIA benchmarks.

Conclusion: Learning reflection-guided correction is an effective paradigm for improving robustness of tool-using VLMs, and the structured reflection data plus local trajectory optimization yields significant gains.

Abstract: Tool-augmented vision-language models (VLMs) can solve multimodal, multi-step tasks by calling external tools, yet they remain fragile in practice. Existing works have two common gaps. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is built mostly on successful trajectories and offers little signal for recovery after tool failures, while sparse trajectory-level RL rewards provide limited guidance on which step failed and how to repair it. We introduce ReGRPO (Reflection-augmented Group Relative Policy Optimization), a framework that learns reflection-guided correction in tool-using agents. ReGRPO starts with a structured reflective data engine: we execute near-miss actions to collect grounded failure observations, then build Reflection-of-Thought triplets (ErrorType, Evidence, FixPlan) paired with corrected actions for warm-start SFT. We then optimize reflection tokens and corrective actions jointly within local trajectories using group-relative advantages, and include a reflection-cost term to reduce unnecessary reflection. Experiments on GTA and GAIA show that, under the same backbone and tool suite, ReGRPO consistently outperforms strong open-source baselines and achieves the best results among the compared open-source controllers. Code and RoT data are available at https://github.com/showlab/ReGRPO.

</details>


### [284] [World-Model Collapse as a Phase Transition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31399)
*Xinyuan Song,Zekun Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Long-horizon language agents undergo a sudden "world-model collapse" at a critical boundary—similar to water boiling—where small parameter changes drastically degrade their internal world representation, making world-model collapse a measurable bottleneck for long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether long-horizon language agents exhibit a phase-transition-like behavior in their implicit world models, where increasing horizon or state load leads to sudden collapse rather than gradual degradation.

Method: A large grid search over state cardinality, dependency density, horizon, branching, observation mode, and mutation rate in a deterministic task family with exact per-step gold state.

Result: A phase diagram emerges with three regimes: a solved plateau, a narrow transition band, and a collapse floor. World-state fidelity fails before action validity, meaning the agent acts from a corrupted world. Stronger models shift but do not eliminate the transition.

Conclusion: World-model collapse is a fundamental, measurable bottleneck for long-horizon agents that cannot be removed by scaling model strength alone.

Abstract: Water looks unchanged as it warms, then at a critical point it boils. We ask whether long-horizon language agents show an analogous transition in their implicit world models. In some parameter settings, changing state load by a small amount, or adding a single step of horizon, leaves behavior nearly unchanged; near a critical boundary, the same small change causes a sudden world collapse. We study this effect in a deterministic task family with exact per-step gold state. A large grid search over state cardinality, dependency density, horizon, branching, observation mode, and mutation rate reveals a phase diagram: a solved plateau, a narrow transition band, and a collapse floor. Per-step traces show the mechanism: world-state fidelity fails before action validity, so the agent is not merely choosing a bad action; it is acting from a corrupted world. Stronger models translate the critical boundary but do not remove the qualitative transition. These results make world-model collapse a measurable bottleneck for long-horizon agents.

</details>


### [285] [Wisdom Of The (AI) Crowd: Investigating Artificial Swarm Intelligence In Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31404)
*Justin Brenne,Christian Meske*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether LLMs can replicate swarm intelligence effects through intra-model sampling and inter-model aggregation, finding up to 37 percentage point error reduction in MAPE across GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4.5. LLMs also demonstrate metacognitive awareness with positive correlations (ρ=0.242-0.568) between confidence width and estimation error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human swarm intelligence is effective but faces scalability issues in cost, coordination, and time. The authors want to know if LLMs can approximate these collective intelligence effects through artificial swarms, filling a gap in understanding AI-based aggregation mechanisms.

Method: Controlled experiment with 960 manually executed prompts across three proprietary models (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5), testing intra-model sampling and inter-model aggregation on eight estimation tasks, measuring error reduction and metacognitive correlations.

Result: Consistent error reduction through aggregation strategies, with up to 37 percentage point improvement in MAPE. Significant positive correlations (Spearman's ρ=0.242-0.568, all p<0.001) between relative confidence interval widths and estimation errors.

Conclusion: LLMs can approximate swarm intelligence effects, possess metacognitive awareness in uncertainty assessment, and should be deployed for organizational decision-making with actionable insights provided.

Abstract: Human swarm intelligence demonstrates remarkable collective accuracy but faces scalability constraints in cost, coordination, and time. We investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can approximate swarm intelligence effects through artificial swarms, addressing a critical gap in understanding AI-based aggregation mechanisms. We conducted a controlled experiment with 960 manually executed prompts across three proprietary models (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5), testing intra-model sampling and inter-model aggregation on eight estimation tasks. Results reveal consistent error reduction through intra- and inter-model aggregation, with significant error reductions up to 37 percentage points in MAPE across different aggregation strategies. We observed small to large effect sizes for positive correlations (Spearman's $ρ=0.242-0.568$, all $p<0.001$) between relative confidence interval widths and relative estimation errors, suggesting LLMs possess metacognitive awareness when assessing uncertainty. We discuss implications for research and practice, providing actionable insights for deploying LLM swarms in organizational decision-making.

</details>


### [286] [Xiaomi-GUI-0 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31410)
*Wanxia Cao,Chengzhen Duan,Pei Fu,Pengzhi Gao,Niu Lian,Fazhan Liu,Hui Liu,Heng Qu,Qinzhuo Wu,Zhehao Yu,Tongbo Chen,Shiqi Cui,Anan Du,Shukai Jia,Yuanfa Li,Yike Liu,Wenchao Lu,Haoyuan Sun,Jiatong Sun,Cheng Tan,Yajie Wang,Changqiao Wu,Tao Xiong,Jiahui Yang,Yuxuan Yuan,Ruoceng Zhang,Shaojie Zhang,Jian Zhu,Jian Luan,Cong Zou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Xiaomi-GUI-0 is a multimodal GUI agent trained on real mobile devices to close the gap between benchmark performance and real-world usability, achieving 72.0% success on RealMobile and 78.9% on AndroidWorld using a three-stage training pipeline with error-driven data flywheel.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GUI agents are trained and evaluated on offline trajectories and simulated environments that differ substantially from real applications in interface layout, interaction logic, and abnormal-state distribution, leading to a persistent gap between benchmark scores and real-world usability.

Method: Proposes a real-device-dominant hybrid infrastructure using physical devices as primary execution environment; constructs multi-source training data (head tasks, long-tail intents, capability-enhancement); introduces error-driven data flywheel converting failures into corrected actions; trains via progressive three-stage pipeline (SFT, step-level RL, agentic RL).

Result: Xiaomi-GUI-0 achieves 72.0% success on RealMobile benchmark and 78.9% on AndroidWorld, with substantial improvements in execution stability and abnormal-state recognition in real-world tasks.

Conclusion: Training GUI agents in a real-device closed loop with error-driven data flywheel and progressive RL pipeline effectively bridges the gap between benchmark scores and real-world usability.

Abstract: Graphical user interface (GUI) agents build on vision-language models to complete user tasks end-to-end in real applications through interface actions such as tapping, swiping, text entry, and navigation. However, existing GUI agents are trained and evaluated largely on offline trajectories, simulated environments, and standardized benchmarks. These differ substantially from real applications in interface layout, interaction logic, and abnormal-state distribution, and cannot faithfully characterize execution stability in real-world use, where account states, permission dialogs, payment authentication, and risk control continually reshape the state distribution and open a persistent gap between benchmark scores and real usability. To close this gap, we propose Xiaomi-GUI-0, a native multimodal GUI agent for real mobile environments, trained and evaluated within a real-device closed loop. At its core is a real-device-dominant hybrid infrastructure, where physical devices are the primary execution environment and sandboxes provide auxiliary support, so that data collection, training, rollout, and evaluation share an execution distribution close to real deployment. We construct multi-source training data spanning high-frequency head tasks, high-generalization data for long-tail intents, and capability-enhancement data for reflection and memory, and introduce an error-driven data flywheel that turns failure trajectories into corrected actions, reflective explanations, and recovery demonstrations. The model is trained through a progressive three-stage pipeline of supervised fine-tuning, step-level reinforcement learning, and agentic reinforcement learning. Evaluated on public benchmarks and our in-house RealMobile, Xiaomi-GUI-0 achieves 72.0% success on RealMobile and 78.9% on AndroidWorld, while substantially improving execution stability and abnormal-state recognition in real-world tasks.

</details>


### [287] [Learning to Select, Not Relearn: Hard-Routed Mixtures of Reasoning LoRAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31413)
*Seyed Alireza Molavi,Zhan Su,Yan Hu,Peyman Sheikholharam Mashhadi,Stefan Byttner,Prayag Tiwari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA composes frozen LoRA reasoning experts via hard top-1 token routing with a straight-through estimator, preserving unit-scale updates while requiring fewer parameters than soft-routing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Composing independently trained LoRA adapters for multi-domain adaptation without sharing original training data is challenging; soft weighted combinations can distort the unit-scale additive updates under which each LoRA module was originally trained.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) domain-specific LoRA adapters are trained independently via RL from verifiable feedback; (2) all experts are frozen, reasoning traces are distilled, and a lightweight shared router with a small attention LoRA is trained using hard top-1 routing with a straight-through estimator.

Result: Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA preserves expert behavior across five benchmarks and multiple model scales, requires substantially fewer trainable parameters than soft-routing mixture baselines, and analysis shows normalized soft mixtures already concentrate most routing mass on a single expert.

Conclusion: Hard unit-scale routing provides a simple and efficient abstraction for composing frozen LoRA experts, matching or outperforming soft-routing approaches with lower parameter cost.

Abstract: Composing independently trained LoRA adapters into a single large language model is useful for multi-domain adaptation, especially when the original training data cannot be shared. A common approach is to use MoE-style routing over LoRA experts, but for frozen pretrained adapters, soft weighted combinations can change the unit-scale additive update under which each LoRA module was originally trained. We propose \textbf{Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA}, a two-stage framework for composing frozen reasoning LoRA experts through unit-scale hard selection. First, domain-specific LoRA adapters are trained independently using reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback to obtain reasoning experts. Then, all experts are frozen, reasoning traces are distilled from them, and only a lightweight shared router together with a small attention LoRA is trained for integration. The router selects exactly one expert per token using hard top-1 routing, while a straight-through estimator enables gradient-based training. Experiments across five benchmarks, multiple model scales, and additional model families show that Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA preserves expert behavior while requiring substantially fewer trainable parameters than soft-routing mixture baselines. Our analysis further shows that normalized soft mixtures often concentrate most routing mass on a single expert, suggesting that hard unit-scale routing provides a simple and efficient abstraction for frozen LoRA expert composition.

</details>


### [288] [BP-TTA: Balanced and Prototype-Guided Test-Time Adaptation in Dynamic Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31420)
*Shaoyang Huang,Yashi Zhu,Yichen Yu,Lei Zhang,Zhang Yi,Tao He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BP-TTA proposes a balanced and prototype-guided test-time adaptation method to handle simultaneous class imbalance and continual domain shifts in dynamic test-time streams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TTA methods fail to account for class imbalance in dynamic scenarios where class imbalance and continual domain shifts occur simultaneously and interact.

Method: BP-TTA combines (1) batch-balanced sampling by integrating current samples with high-confidence historical instances to mitigate bias, and (2) prototype-guided adaptation using evolving class prototypes as constraints to improve pseudo-label reliability.

Result: BP-TTA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art TTA methods in dynamic test-time streaming settings.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses the dual challenges of class imbalance and continual domain shifts in realistic test-time scenarios.

Abstract: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) enables models trained on a source domain to adapt online to unlabeled test data under distribution shifts. While recent TTA methods have moved beyond static settings and begun to consider continual domain shifts, they primarily address distribution drift and fail to account for class imbalance in dynamic scenarios. In real-world test-time streams, class imbalance and continual domain shifts often occur at the same time and interact with each other. In this paper, we propose a novel Balanced and Prototype-Guided Test-Time Adaptation (BP-TTA) method, which combines batch-balanced sampling with prototype-guided adaptation to handle the class imbalance and continual domain shift problems. BP-TTA constructs balanced adaptation batches by integrating current samples with high-confidence historical instances, effectively mitigating bias toward dominant classes and stabilizing online updates. Meanwhile, BP-TTA maintains evolving class prototypes during inference and leverages prototype similarity as a constraint for model adaptation, thereby improving the reliability of pseudo-labels and enhancing the stability of online updates under persistent domain shifts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BP-TTA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art TTA methods in dynamic test-time streaming settings.

</details>


### [289] [Ask the World Before Acting: Budgeted Environment Probing for World-Model Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31422)
*Xinyuan Song,Zekun Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a budgeted probing mechanism where language agents can query the environment to repair their world model during long-horizon tasks, analyzing how different belief types (procedural vs. spatial) benefit differently from such probes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon language agents suffer from world model drift across decisions, causing failures that originate before the failing action. Environment interaction should be treated as a scarce calibration resource, not just task advancement.

Method: The authors introduce a budgeted probing operator for structured belief tables, and conduct a type-stratified analysis distinguishing procedural beliefs (tool dependencies) from spatial beliefs (object locations, graph edges) to formalize the probe-action frontier.

Result: Mid-planning environment evidence reduces terminal world-model error when the probe policy follows the task structure. Procedural beliefs benefit from targeted checks, while spatial beliefs rely more on structural cues.

Conclusion: A calibration-aware probe policy aligned with belief type and task structure significantly improves long-horizon agent reliability.

Abstract: Long-horizon language agents do not only choose actions; they carry a private model of the world from one decision to the next. When that model drifts, a later failure can be decided before the failing action is ever taken. We study a direct repair mechanism: before committing to the next task action, an agent may ask the environment about one belief field and write the answer back into its world model. This makes environment interaction a scarce calibration resource, not merely a way to advance the task. We introduce \method, a budgeted probing operator for structured belief tables. The useful probes are not the same everywhere. Procedural beliefs, such as tool dependencies, can often be repaired by targeted checks, but those checks spend steps that the task may need. Spatial beliefs, such as object locations and graph edges, rely more on structural cues; the agent's own confidence can be a poor guide when the world changes off-screen. A type-stratified analysis formalizes this probe-action frontier, and controlled experiments show that mid-planning environment evidence reduces terminal world-model error when the probe policy follows the structure of the task.

</details>


### [290] [CDR-Bench: Evaluating Faithful Execution of Compositional, Order-Sensitive Data Refinement Recipes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31435)
*Yuchen Huang,Xiang Li,Zhenqing Ling,Sijia Li,Qianli Shen,Daoyuan Chen,Yi R. Fung,Yaliang Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces CDR-Bench, a benchmark of 3,462 tasks for testing LLMs on compositional, order-sensitive data refinement. Experiments with 10+ LLMs show they struggle significantly with compositional and order-sensitive recipes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks either isolate text editing or entangle it with code/tool execution, making it unclear whether LLMs can faithfully execute compositional, order-sensitive data refinement recipes.

Method: The authors constructed CDR-Bench with 3,462 high-quality tasks across 4 real-world domains and 29 operators, evaluating models under atomic, order-agnostic, and order-sensitive settings with deterministic reference outputs for exact evaluation.

Result: Experiments on 10+ state-of-the-art LLMs reveal consistent failure patterns: performance degrades sharply in compositional settings, and order-sensitive recipe success collapses.

Conclusion: Current LLMs lack the procedural faithfulness required for reliable compositional data refinement.

Abstract: Data refinement involves executing multi-step recipes over evolving text states, where both composition and execution order of processing operators determine the outcome. While existing benchmarks either isolate text editing or entangle it with code and tool execution, it remains unclear whether LLMs can directly and faithfully execute these compositional, order-sensitive data refinement recipes. To fill this gap, we introduce CDR-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring 3,462 high-quality tasks spanning four real-world data refinement domains and 29 distinct operators. Our benchmark evaluates models across atomic, order-agnostic, and order-sensitive settings, leveraging deterministic reference outputs to enable exact evaluation. Experiments on 10+ state-of-the-art LLMs reveal consistent failure patterns: performance degrades sharply in compositional settings, and order-sensitive recipe success collapses. These findings underline that current LLMs lack the procedural faithfulness required for reliable compositional data refinement.

</details>


### [291] [Who Determines the Meaning of an Emotion? Affective Sovereignty as an Epistemic Consequence of Measurement Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31442)
*Keito Inoshita*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces the concept of the "Affectosphere" (societal-scale emotion computing), defines an "epistemic gap" showing that emotion AI cannot recover the meaning of individual emotions due to irreducible uncertainty, and argues for a norm of "affective sovereignty" — the experiencing subject retains final interpretive authority over their own emotions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emotion-sensing AI is being deployed widely (vehicles, homes, dialogue agents, infrastructure), yet the fundamental normative question of who has authority to determine the meaning of one's emotion remains underexplored.

Method: The authors define a "meaning distribution" (labels from annotators under a fixed protocol), decompose uncertainty into reducible and irreducible components, and mathematically demonstrate that irreducible uncertainty for individual instances cannot be estimated with adequate coverage under realistic annotator counts — the "epistemic gap."

Result: High device confidence does not constitute evidence that irrecoverable meaning has been recovered. The irreducible component of meaning distributions cannot be adequately estimated at the individual level.

Conclusion: Final interpretive authority over one's emotion is procedurally reserved for the experiencing subject (affective sovereignty). Emotion AI design, evaluation, and regulation should prioritize explicit allocation of interpretive authority over accuracy maximization.

Abstract: Emotion-sensing AI is rapidly becoming embedded in vehicles, home appliances, dialogue agents, and social infrastructure, giving rise to a sphere in which emotion is no longer confined to individual experience but is instead observed and computed at a societal scale, a domain we term the Affectosphere. Yet a central normative question in this domain has remained underexplored: who has the final authority to determine the meaning of one's own emotion? This study addresses the question from the epistemological side of measurement's structural limits. We define a meaning distribution as the distribution of labels assigned by annotators drawn from a population under a fixed annotation protocol, and decompose its uncertainty into reducible and irreducible components. We then demonstrate that, while emotion AI can assign high-confidence point labels and discriminate real differences at an aggregate level, the irreducible component of the meaning distribution for individual instances cannot be estimated with adequate coverage under realistic annotator counts, a systematic divergence we term the epistemic gap. The key finding is that high device confidence does not constitute evidence that irrecoverable meaning has been recovered. From this epistemic gap, together with an explicitly stated normative premise, namely that the output of a system which cannot recover a quantity in principle must not be treated as its authoritative determination, we derive the norm that the final interpretive authority over the meaning of one's emotion is procedurally reserved for the experiencing subject, the norm of affective sovereignty. These results suggest that the design, evaluation, and regulation of emotion AI should place explicit allocation of interpretive authority, rather than accuracy maximisation, at their core.

</details>


### [292] [CSTrader: A Testbed for Language-Grounded Trading in a Community-Driven Virtual Asset Market](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.31461)
*Yao Shi,Kingfung Luo,Nan Tang,Yuyu Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CSTrader is a multi-agent LLM framework for trading CS2 weapon skins that outperforms baselines by integrating technical analysis, liquidity, sentiment, and risk management, achieving up to 7.58% return during a market downturn.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Niche asset markets like CS2 skins are volatile and language-driven, making them hard for traditional models but ideal for studying how LLMs can convert unstructured text into trading decisions.

Method: CSTrader uses a multi-agent framework that integrates heterogeneous signals, specialized agents (technical, liquidity, events, reversed sentiment), risk control, transaction friction, and portfolio management to produce buy/sell/hold decisions.

Result: CSTrader outperformed the falling market index (-15.62%) and single-prompt LLM baselines, achieving up to 7.58% cumulative return with controlled risk.

Conclusion: Liquidity, reversed sentiment, and transaction friction agents are essential for stable profits in language-driven niche markets, which serve as useful benchmarks for language-to-action research.
